     Trying to reengineer learning
 without being versed in the basics
 of learning is like trying to improve
brain surgery without knowing much
            about the brain.

                – Breakthrough Leadership
                       in the Digital Age 45
CHAPTER 3


Learning Engineering
is Human-Centered
by Khanh-Phuong!Thai, Scotty!D.!Craig, Jim!Goodell,
Jodi!Lis, Jordan!Richard!Schoenherr, and Janet Kolodner




Learning engineering is a process and practice that applies
the learning sciences using human-centered engineering
design methodologies and data-informed decision-
making to support learners and their development.



What’s “human-centered” within learning engineering?
Human-centered engineering design means designing from the perspective of hu-
mans who will be interacting with implemented designs.1 Learning engineers design
from the perspective of the learners who will engage with designed products:
      • What will keep learners engaged and motivated over time?
      • What support do they need to learn well?
      • How can those supports be provided in ways that simultaneously
        support learners’ mental model building along with maintaining
        their interest, motivation, and engagement and also foster
        positive attitudes, emotions, and identity development?
These are only some of the questions that must be answered to produce effective and
engaging designs that result in engaged and productive learning.
      Establishing deep connections with targeted learners can be difficult. Chapter 2
lays out some of the complexities of how people develop understanding of concepts
and flexible capabilities, and it describes many of the different cognitive, social,
emotional, cultural, and other factors that affect learning. We know that learning is


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-7
84 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     not simply “tea steeping.” That is, we can’t assume that learners will simply acquire
     knowledge over time as they listen to lectures, work on problem sets, and engage
     in other learning activities. When learning engineers design for learners and their
     learning, they must consider the knowledge, values, beliefs, life experiences, and
     strategies learners bring with them into the learning environment and the difficulties
     inherent in making the inferences necessary for deep understanding and competency
     development. We need to use that understanding to design in ways that invite learners
     in, keep them wanting to participate, and support their learning.
            The learning engineering process (as outlined in Chapter 1) starts with under-
     standing the challenge or problem to be solved and the context in which learning will
     take place. Investigation is needed before beginning to design in order to come to know
     learners’ interests, capabilities, developmental stages, probable prior knowledge,
     everyday experiences, beliefs, support needs, sometimes their living conditions, and
     perhaps other things about them. Investigation also needs to include knowing those
     same things about the facilitators or teachers who will aid the learning process as
     well as the opportunities and constraints presented by the learning context itself (for
     example, the kinds of physical venues learning will take place in and the capabilities
     and beliefs of people who are available to learners in those contexts). Similarly, the
     investigation should also examine the capabilities and constraints imposed by the
     variety of solution stakeholders (for example, the organizations offering the learning
     experiences, the people and organizations available to participate in design, budget,
     and due dates). This knowledge, learned through the initial investigation, defines the
     challenge, and it’s used continuously during design, including when:
           •   Developing potential designs and pieces of designs
           •   Critiquing potential designs and refining them
           •   Deciding between possible designs and design choices
           •   Choosing participants for testing designs
           •   Designing assessments, evaluation approaches, and instruments
     This is done best in conjunction with stakeholders, including learners, teachers or
     other facilitators, those who set policy, and representatives of community organiza-
     tions that may be involved in the designed solution.
            The learning engineering process includes design as a part of the creation
     phase, which itself includes ideation as well as refining, critiquing, culling, and
     choosing among ideas. This, too, is a human-centered process and requires working
     closely with a variety of stakeholders, many of the same ones who collaborate in
     understanding the challenge. Stakeholders who have experienced the challenges
                                      Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 85



being addressed have valuable insights.
       Coming to understand the challenge is an iterative process. Designers do their
best to learn early in the process, but as ideas are developed, design teams must learn
more before making setting requirements. If relevant stakeholders are engaged with
the design team, they often can provide answers to questions that were not addressed
earlier in the process.
       Testing also must be driven in a human-centered way. The learning engineer-
ing team must identify subgroups of the targeted population who should be included
in testing, indicators of progress toward targeted goals, and how to measure progress.
Here, too, stakeholder experience and understanding can help professional learning
engineers identify indicators and procedures for measurement.
       To accomplish all of this, the learning engineering process borrows from
many methodologies, including human-centered design,2 design thinking,3 univer-
sal design for learning,4 learning experience design,5 design-based research,6 and
engineering design.7 At its core, the best designs for learning are informed by pre-
dictions about how learners will interact and function within a designed learning
environment. They’re iteratively tested for efficacy, and data are collected about
learners and elements of their learning environment. (More specifics about bringing
a learner-centered approach to testing through data instrumentation and analysis
can be found in Chapters 5 and 6.) What’s learned from those data is used to refine
designs and design elements.
       In the rest of this chapter, we’ll explore various human-centered approaches
that inform learning engineering. Then we’ll present narratives of three learning
engineering design efforts and how each group addressed challenges inherent in
designing effective learning experiences for their targeted learners.


                                        !!"
A Human-Centered Approach to Learning Engineering
Human-centered design requires at least the following six activities: 8
      1. Observation, consulting the appropriate research literature, and
         interviews to understand the target end users and their environment.
      2. Ideation, coming up with options for addressing the challenge, focuses
         on designing toward what’s learned through those investigations.
86 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           3. Rapid prototyping is done to make design ideas concrete so they can be
              presented to end users and other stakeholders for comment and testing.
           4. User testing around the prototype is done with the purpose of collecting
              preference and usability data from end users.
           5. Redesign of ideas and prototypes improves on the prototype based on
              those data.
           6. The process is repeated using iteratively more sophisticated prototypes,
              increasingly real environments, and an increasingly diverse set of end
              users and other stakeholders until a satisfactory solution is achieved.
     Through this process, the solution is implemented robustly for use in the real world.
     Human-centered design is informed by and informs approaches to design that come
     from particular disciplines, and some of the particulars of each of those are also im-
     portant to good design for learners and learning. These related approaches include
     user-centered design, user-experience design, and human-systems integration.


     USER-CENTERED DESIGN

     User-centered design is an approach to designing software to make interactions
     between the software and those using it fluid and productive.


     User-centered design has its foundations in the design of human-computer inter-
     faces. Its focus is on the interactions end users will have with software systems.
     Nielsen and Molich asserted that there are “five golden rules” when approaching
     user interface (UI) design: 9
           1. Knowing the end users of the system is critical.
           2. Users should be involved in the design process.
           3. The user interface must be taken into consideration.
           4. Usability is defined, criteria for measuring usability are identified, and
              measurement data are collected and evaluated to assess those criteria.
           5. The design process should iteratively address and fix usability issues.
     The design process is an iterative process of research, design, and user testing, with
     all three focusing on users’ wants and needs. All three components of the process
     might be repeated in each iteration; design and user testing are always repeated.
                                      Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 87



USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN

User experience design focuses on designing experiences that people want to con-
tinue to engage in and return to repeatedly.


User experience (UX) design is rooted in user-centered design but focuses on the
experiences people will have while engaging with the implemented design.10 UX
design focuses on end-user satisfaction and those things that affect how fluidly users
can engage with the designed system. Good UX design:
      •   Is iterative
      •   Includes research, design, and testing
      •   Involves end users in the design process
      •   Requires defining what satisfaction and fluidity mean
      •   Includes criteria and measuring of the extent to which users are satisfied
Game designers use UX design and employ many tricks of the trade for fostering
excited engagement.11 For the gaming industry, end user satisfaction means that
users will continue to return to the game and engage with it and will recommend
it to others. Fluidity has to do with the ease with which users can carry out the
necessary interactions and move on to the next ones. Satisfaction might be measured
by collecting data about time devoted to play, frequency of play, and, perhaps,
participation in web discussions about the game, and the nature of those discussions.
Fluidity might be measured by collecting keystrokes and their timing and looking in
web discussions for content associated with fluidity.
       To design for learner engagement, learning engineers might take advantage
of some of the tricks that game designers use. They might measure satisfaction of
learners engaged in a learning experience by collecting data from discussions learners
are having with each other, the frequency of their participation in learning activities,
their frequency of engagement, and their engagement over time. Particular data to
be collected, of course, will depend on the nature of the learning experience and
the venue in which learners participate. We discuss more about learning-experience
design below. See Chapter 5 to learn about a data collection framework for games
applied to learning, and look at Chapter 15 for a brief discussion on some tricks game
designers use to keep players engaged that can be applied to learning experiences.
88 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     HUMAN-SYSTEMS INTEGRATION

     Human-systems integration focuses on people and systems performing together to
     achieve a set of goals.


     Human-systems integration is an approach applied to system development
     that seeks to optimize total system performance. It’s a philosophy and series of
     processes designed to bring human-centered principles into traditional systems
     engineering.12 Human-systems integration is typically applied to large systems
     that include hardware, software, organizational processes, human personnel, and
     potentially other components (like facilities) that all operate together. It emphasizes
     developing systems that effectively and affordably take advantage of the capabilities
     and limitations of humans.13 Human-systems integration also focuses on a system’s
     life cycle, from its initial conception, through its development and operation, and
     even its eventual sunsetting. Core tenets of human-systems integration include the
     following: 14
           •   Focus on human performance throughout the design process
           •   Optimize the system as a whole, not just as individual components
           •   Maximize benefits while balancing risks and costs
           •   Use multidisciplinary design, including both designers and stakeholders
            Learning engineers designing highly immersive learning experiences may
     need to take into account this full set of tenets. See, for example, the Connected
     Worlds installation at the New York Hall of Science.15 The learning experience
     happens in the context of a highly complex full-room embodied interactive
     simulation. People engaging with the simulation are learning from it; at the same
     time, every engagement they have with it needs to seem instantaneously responsive.
     The design of the system included specialized hardware and software designers,
     learning scientists, learning experience designers, science education experts, and
     science educators. In addition to the traditional learning components, the team needed
     to make a wide range of human-system design decisions, including environmental
     safety, technology maintainability, and development of training for the installation
     staff on how to use and maintain the system.
                                       Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 89




                                         !""
Designing for Variation Across Learners
As Chapter 2 points out, individual learners within a population or cohort differ
in a huge variety of ways that are important to consider when supporting their
learning. They’ve lived different experiences resulting in different zones of proximal
development. They bring different capabilities and foundational mental models to
their learning. They’ve been exposed to different ways of doing things. They have
different interests, different dispositions, and so forth.
       The best educational approaches will work for the vast majority of learners in
a targeted population. Put simply, a good middle-school math curriculum will catch
the interest and support the learning of the vast majority of middle schoolers. A good
piece of EdTech designed to teach storytelling will support the vast majority of those
for whom it’s aimed (whether middle schoolers or second-language learners).
       The same learning activities won’t work for learners of entirely different de-
velopmental levels, interests, or capabilities. Appropriate scope is critical to making
a human-centered approach work, as is determining how much of the variation
across a population can be accommodated in a design. But even within more target-
ed populations, there will always be a wide variety of differences to address. This
means learning engineers need to investigate (before designing), identifying factors
and their variations that will affect learning among the identified population. You
certainly have to identify the most common variations, and you’ll have to design
toward some less common ones as well. Some readers may wonder how to possibly
address the huge variations in capabilities and the support needs across a population.
This is among the biggest challenges that learning engineering teams face.
       Don Norman, a leader in the field of designing human-computer interfaces,
also pointed out a subtle challenge in human-centered design regarding population
variety.16 He argued that no single design can address the needs of all users, and that
if design teams aren’t careful, then human-centered approaches can bias solutions
toward the group participating in its creation. Norman further warned that if a de-
sign isn’t tested with a varied enough set of end users, then this bias will get stronger
across iterations. In the years since Norman pointed out this tension, a variety of
approaches have been developed that focus on addressing the needs of a large por-
tion of the selected population. These include design thinking, participatory design,
and design justice.
90 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     DESIGN THINKING

     Design thinking emphasizes the need for empathy with the population, such as
     recognizing their knowledge, capabilities, needs, and wants.


     Brown and Wyatt define the design thinking process as a combination of inspiration,
     ideation, and implementation.17 The inspiration phase starts with framing and
     scoping the problem and then gathering and synthesizing data. The ideation phase
     takes those data and uses them to generate ideas for possible solutions. Then, an
     implementation phase converges on a solution, builds a prototype, tests, and refines
     the prototype with the focus on delivering the product. The three components of
     the process are interwoven with one another rather than occurring as individual
     steps—the whole process and each component are iterative. While the basic human-
     centered design process (above) was articulated to design physical objects to be
     used by people, design thinking extends design practice into designing processes,
     systems of all kinds, and services. Design thinking practitioners focus on designing
     their products based on what potential users want and need.18
            Design thinking puts great focus on understanding why people do the things
     they do and how they interact with each other. A significant component of the
     inspiration phase includes getting to know or empathizing with the population.
     Design thinking researchers and practitioners have developed sophisticated methods
     and practices for building empathy with the population, for example, learning about
     end users and their interactions in environments. These tools include such techniques
     as personas, customer journey maps, service blueprints, business model innovation,
     and rapid prototyping. Some of these are discussed later in this chapter, and while it’s
     beyond our scope to cover all of them in detail, an overview can be found in Chapter
     12, Human-Centered Design Tools. More recommendations are also available in the
     paper “Design Thinking Methods and Tools for Innovation” from the Design, User
     Experience, and Usability Conference.19
                                      Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 91



PARTICIPATORY DESIGN

Participatory design tells us to include end users, those who know end users well,
and other experts on our design teams.


Participatory design,20 also called co-design,21 focuses on the design team and on
methods for actively engaging the broad range of stakeholders in the design process.
Each of the human-centered approaches in this chapter emphasizes the need to
include members of the targeted user community or learning population in the
design process. Participatory design adds that it isn’t enough to simply involve those
people in the design process; rather, it’s necessary to identify all stakeholders and
actively engage representatives from each stakeholder group in the design process.22
Two common challenges for professional designers are identifying ways of actively
engaging stakeholders who aren’t educated designers and treating these design team
members as integral teammates who bring important expertise.23
       Most of the approaches discussed earlier assume there’s a client who is
contracting with a design team to address a challenge. Participatory design begins
earlier: professional designers and all stakeholders work together to articulate the
challenge and decide which of its aspects to take on, for example, they work together
to identify requirements. They then continue working together to develop solutions.
Participatory design is conceived as having three basic stages that mostly overlap
with the process components of other approaches: initial exploration of work,
discovery processes, and prototyping.24

   ► Initial exploration of work: All stakeholders meet to share ideas and
      viewpoints on the situation being investigated and together develop a process
      for coming to understand the situation. This may include methods such as
      observations, interviews, walk-throughs and organizational visits, and
      examinations of artifacts.
   ► Discovery processes: After data are collected, the team clarifies goals and
      values and agrees on desired outcomes. The goal is to make meaning out of
      the problem solutions rather than to simply describe them.
   ► Prototyping and testing: Professional designers and stakeholders collaborate
      to shape project artifacts to fit into the envisioned problem solution.

The whole thing is an iterative process that involves multiple rounds of design and
feedback.
92 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     DESIGN JUSTICE

     Design justice reminds us to make sure we identify ethnic, racial, class, gender,
     disability, and other variations across our population, that we recognize intersec-
     tions between these, and that we don’t disadvantage particular subgroups more
     than we disadvantage other subgroups.


     Design justice is the newest of these approaches. It seeks to challenge structural
     inequalities and systems of oppression.25 Design justice urges us to consider how our
     designs distribute benefits and burdens and to examine issues of power, penalty, and
     privilege as we design. This enables designers to be more accountable to those who
     are intersectionally disadvantaged (for example, at the intersecting experiences of
     gender, sexual orientation, class, race, ethnicity, disability, and immigration status)
     and to prioritize the needs of those most affected by our designs. Design justice
     requires the following:

        ► In addition to being participatory, our design teams must include designers
           across identities (for example, women, Black communities), as well as the
           intersections (for example, based on race, gender, ethnicity, migration status,
           disability) of those who will be affected by our designs.
        ► We should make sure to iteratively test our designs with members of those
           different groups.
        ► We need to keep track of power relationships as we design (they suggest
           Patricia Hill Collins’ matrix of domination to do this) and design to challenge
           systems of oppression.
        ► We need to be proactive and systematic in inviting people from disadvantaged
           groups to use and critique our designs.

            As well, design justice tenets remind us that anybody can participate meaning-
     fully in design teams if opportunities for participation are designed appropriately.
     Design justice is discussed in more detail in Chapter 7, and the KNO example later
     in this chapter shows participation by community members on design teams.
                                      Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 93




                                        !#"
Designing for Learners and Learning
Learning solutions can immediately use many of the general techniques already
discussed. However, in addition to these, more learning-focused principles and
processes should be considered. Learner-centered design has its roots in user-
centered design but is concerned with learners who will learn from the implementation
of a design rather than on users who will use a design.26 Learner-centered design
also focuses on the design of learning technologies. Several attributes differentiate
computer-aided learners from typical computer users, such as: 27
      1.   Learners have low expertise and capabilities in the task domain
      2.   Learners change in interesting ways during their use of the system
      3.   Learners will be motivated to engage in learning in very diverse ways
      4.   Learners are quite diverse in the support they need for learning
      5.   The most usable computer interactions aren’t always the best ways to
           learn the targeted concepts and skills
      Learner-centered design emphasizes tries to build solutions for the diversity of
learners, in ways that address the gulf between their knowledge and capabilities and
those of an expert, all while keeping learners engaged over time. Learner-centered
design teams include subject-matter experts, teachers, designers, and developers and
they consider usability and learning outcomes. Since the introduction of learner-
centered design, a variety of approaches have added detail to the considerations
involved in designing for learners and learning. These include universal design for
learning, learning experience design, and design-based research.


UNIVERSAL DESIGN FOR LEARNING

Universal design for learning ensures that learners with disabilities and other vari-
ations are considered during the design process.


Universal design for learning began as a framework for designing curriculum ma-
terials and learning technologies so that those with disabilities can also interact with
them effectively and easily.28 More recently, it’s branched out to focus on all kinds of
variations across learners, including social justice and equity issues that go beyond
94 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     disability. Universal design for learning had two foci: (a) providing flexibility in the
     ways information is presented and the ways learners are asked to engage, and (b)
     reducing barriers to learning by providing proper supports. Its goal is to maintain
     high achievement expectations for all learners regardless of disability status.29 As
     required in other human-centered design approaches, it also requires involving end
     users (in this case, those with targeted disabilities) in the design process.
            Ziho Kang is an Associate Professor of Industrial and Systems Engineering
     at the University of Oklahoma. He and colleagues articulated several guidelines for
     accomplishing these goals: 30

        ► Teachers should appropriately and strategically display information using a
           variety of representations, for example, analogy, multiple examples, and using
           a variety of media.
        ► Learners should be able to choose from a variety of modalities for expressing
           themselves and demonstrating what they know and are capable of.
        ► All learners should have a way to engage in every assignment. This means
           that what’s assigned must be doable in some way by all students; there may,
           for example, be different levels of solving a problem so that all can feel
           accomplished, nobody will be overly frustrated, and those who can take on
           more challenges have opportunities to do so.

     Recently, proponents of universal design for learning have realized that many of the
     recommendations they make about designing for those with disabilities also hold
     when designing for equity across those who bring different knowledge, experiences,
     and capabilities to the classroom.


     LEARNING EXPERIENCE DESIGN

     Learning experience design focuses on designing for adult learners and integrates
     instructional design, psychology, neuroscience, design thinking, and UX into its
     methods.


     Learning experience design reflects another broad set of approaches that combine
     elements of adult learning and development, instructional design, psychology,
     neuroscience, design thinking, and UX design.31 Learning experience design can be
     thought of as bringing together UX design and interactive media design principles to
                                      Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 95



design educational experiences.32 Walcutt and Malone claim it’s informed, as well,
by industrial knowledge design, experience design, experiential learning, behavioral
economics, and human-systems integration.33
       When adopting a learning experience design approach to design, designers
should focus on the ultimate goal of the learning experience (such as improving
workplace productivity with up-skilling training versus simply fostering new skills
in employees) as well as the larger context around it. Designers should use holistic
approaches to user-centered design, borrowed from many files, and design holistic
experiences that consider form, function, and aesthetics as well as address senses,
emotions, cognition, personal connections and engagement, and social identity. These
designs should be robust despite the inevitable errors and biases that each person
using the solution will have. And learning experience design encourages designers
to think of a learning solution as a collective system, rather than unconnected events,
learning activities, or design decisions.34


DESIGN-BASED RESEARCH

Design-based research derives from research in the learning sciences and is a
methodology for iteratively making designs better while at the same time adding to
our body of knowledge learning.


At its most basic, design-based research begins with a design that’s informed by
what’s known about how people learn and how to support learning. Designers keep
track of each of the design decisions they make and develop a logic model showing
how the design’s different components are meant to affect and contribute to one
another. Rich data descriptions are collected as the design is being carried out, and
these allow the designers to compare their predictions to what happens in real-world
practice.
       Learning outcomes are usually part of the real-world data collection, and
these are augmented with important snapshots of what learners are doing and
saying. Comparing the designers’ expectations to what’s actually happening during
a learning experience or activity gives insight into what’s happening as expected
(or not as expected) as well as other surprises. Explanations are derived for any
unexpected results, and then refinements are suggested. In cases where explanations
can’t be derived or where not enough is known to suggest refinements, researchers
might be able to identify holes in the theories they’re using. They might engage
96 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     in additional research before moving on and may even, through the data they’ve
     collected, be able to suggest new approaches to the professional community. While
     learning engineers don’t necessarily engage in academic research, new scholarly
     findings on how to better support learning might very well be derivable from a
     learning engineering effort.
            Design-based implementation research is similar to design-based research
     but focuses on adapting designed learning activities for particular contexts.35 For
     example, a curriculum that’s been designed for the general middle-school population
     may need adjustments to work well at a school with a different culture, teachers with
     different backgrounds, or students with different resources than was assumed by
     designers. The same iterative process used in design-based research is used to adapt
     a design for use in special contexts.
            Three big ideas that underlie design-based research (and design-based imple-
     mentation research) are important for learning engineering.

        ► What happens during a learning experience or set of learning experiences
           affects what will be learned by the end. Therefore, evaluation of complex
           designs requires a protocol that lends itself to knowing our intentions and how
           well the design meets them in practice.
        ► Design-based research tells us what design process data to keep track of
           while designing so that issues deep within a component of a complex learning
           experience can be tracked down for refinement.
        ► Design-based research provides us with a method, called conjecture mapping,36
           for keeping track of refinements and reasons for them over time that then
           allows guidelines for supporting learning to be derived and refined.

             We don’t go into detail about conjecture mapping in this chapter (instead
     refer to Chapter 12, Human-Centered Design Tools). Suffice it to say, it’s no small
     undertaking keeping track of all of the design decisions and why they were made,
     how design components interact, expected observable outputs of different design
     components, and how those components together contribute to the results. Tracking
     all of that and the results of using designs in a real-world setting and how interpretation
     of those results inform futures iterations is an even bigger undertaking. Conjecture
     mapping suggests a framework for doing that. (See also Chapter 4, which includes
     some advice about using models for keeping track of complex and complicated
     interactions between parts of designs.)
                                       Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 97




                                         !$"
User-Centered Design Techniques
User- and learner-centered design approaches contribute several methods that are
important to learning engineering, including:
      • Personas as a way of keeping track of variations in the population
      • Prototyping for quickly getting feedback from a community on ideas
      • End user interactions for getting empirical feedback on design decisions
Each of these tools is used in the case studies described a little later in the chapter.


PERSONAS

A persona is a description of a subgroup of people who will interact with one’s
designs or some representative of that subgroup. In other words, a persona is a de-
scription of a fictitious user or learner. One persona is created for each of the major
subgroups of potential users who will interact with the design being created. When
designing for learners and learning, this means creating one to represent each of the
major variations across members of the targeted learner population.
       The set of personas is used during design to decide which of several options
will work better and to guide adaption so that those options work more broadly.
Personas are used during critique, to predict how broadly a design will work, and
they’re used during testing to identify those subpopulations with whom a design
should be tested.
       Personas are developed using data that are collected before design begins,
during the investigative empathy stage. Personas may be the most important
resources created for guiding design: The best designs take personas into account
during ideation, critiquing and idea refinement, and development of test procedures.
The first case study below is from the Age of Learning’s Math Readiness. That
example includes a persona that was developed through interactions with fifty
children, two and three years old, and it illustrates how the persona was used to
guide ideation and idea refinement.
       Included in each persona is a name, photo or sketch, and possible behaviors,
goals, interests, and frustrations—all as learned through data collection. The design
justice approach emphasizes that personas should be created based on the lived ex-
periences of end users (learners in our case) and other stakeholders and not just on
98 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     imagining what their capabilities, needs, and frustrations are. Personas should also
     include other demographic and psychographic information that might affect the ways
     people will interact with or participate in an implemented design.37 Development of
     personas is iterative; they’re refined over time as the team’s understanding evolves.


     PROTOTYPING

     Design ideas can be critiqued, tested, refined, and extended only if they are concrete
     enough so that everyone on the design team, stakeholders, clients, and end users,
     all have the same understanding of a design in progress. This is done through
     prototyping. A prototype brings design ideas to life, providing the certainty
     needed to judge the extent to which requirements are addressed.38 Prototypes might
     represent a design statically or may be created to simulate actions of and interactions
     with a proposed product. One of the adages of the design thinking approach is to
     “prototype early and often.” Making a design idea concrete through a prototype
     allows discovery of design issues early in the process, providing the design team
     opportunities to get support from stakeholders and clients early in the process so
     that necessary refinements of vision and scope can be made early in the life cycle.
     Prototypes range from low-fidelity paper prototypes and wireframes to high-fidelity
     mock-ups that give a visual representation of the system, to interactive high-fidelity
     prototypes that look and act like the actual system but with the necessary code or
     functionality needed to answer questions relevant to the current cycle. Prototyping
     is done for each iterative cycle, with appropriate end users and stakeholders using
     each successive prototype to critique or test the design in progress. The value of
     prototyping is illustrated in the three examples below.


     END USER INTERACTIONS

     End user interactions should happen early and often in the iterative design process.39
     Early on, to check their assumptions and initial ideas, the design team develops
     empathy by getting to know the user population and the context of use. Later, the
     design team interacts with end users to do end-user testing of designs in progress.
     Interactions with end users, whether to understand them or to test designs in progress
     can be accomplished in a variety of ways, including: 40
           • User interviews, or one-on-one interviews with members of a population
           • Surveys and questionnaires that uncover end users’ needs and wants
                                      Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 99



      • Focus groups where a facilitator asks end users a set of questions
      • Card sorting where end users organize topics into meaningful categories
      • Contextual inquiry where a researcher visits end users in their own
        environments to learn about their common practices, interests, and so on


                                        !%"
The case studies in this section and the one following it show how two organizations
have used the suggestions made by the many methodologies listed. The first section
(immediately below) includes the educational game Math Readiness and highlights
empathy, personas, iterative refinement, and end-user testing.


Age of Learning, Inc.’s Math Readiness
Age of Learning, Inc. is a provider of digital learning resources for children ages two
through thirteen. One of those learning resources is My Math Academy, an adaptive
learning game system to help children from pre-kindergarten to second grade (ages
four to eight) build a strong foundation in mathematics. At the time of this story, the
design team for My Math Academy had been working on the set of games for over
five years.
       Working with research partners, the My Math Academy team evaluated its
effectiveness in helping children of their targeted population learn math. They found
that while My Math Academy accelerated math learning in older pre-kindergarteners
(four-and-a-half upon entering pre-K) and kindergarteners and increased their
engagement, interest, and confidence in learning math,41 it was not as effective
with younger preschoolers (not yet four at the beginning of the school year and the
youngest four-year-olds). These children, they found, were missing prerequisite skills
and knowledge needed to benefit from interactions with My Math Academy games.42
The company took this as a call for the development of Math Readiness, a series of
games designed to address mathematics readiness. Math Readiness would be used to
prepare preschoolers to advance to My Math Academy. The target population, they
decided, would be two- and three-year-olds.
       The My Math Academy team, who also were tasked with designing Math
Readiness, is interdisciplinary; its members have expertise in learning sciences,
design research, curriculum development, game design, engineering, art and ani-
mation, learning analytics, and efficacy research. The development process followed
three phases: exploration, iteration, and validation.43 They worked collaboratively
100 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     throughout the phases using Agile development methodologies (see Chapter 11),
     which calls for nimbly iterating to explore a variety of design concepts, test proto-
     types, and validate designs. This case study first presents the process of learning
     about two- and three-year-olds and then the design process for one of the ten games
     that make up Math Readiness. Over the course of getting to know two- and three-
     year-olds and then designing this game, the team conducted nine interview sessions
     with over fifty two- and three-year-olds, some for learning about two- and three-
     year-old children before beginning design and some to test ideas in progress.


     EXPLORATION PHASE

     What does it mean to be two or three years old? The goal of the exploration phase
     was to understand the challenge to be solved and the learner population. The team’s
     approach was grounded in the design thinking concept of empathy. They aimed to
     learn the needs, interests, and capabilities of the children they were designing for:
     Who are these children? How and why do they behave the way they do? What math
     readiness skills do they need to learn? How do they learn? Where and when do they
     learn? How might we engage them in learning? How do we know if and what they
     are learning? This included consulting the research literature on children of this
     age, interacting with children of this age to learn more specifics, and developing a
     persona.
            Exploration began by gathering information from the research literature on
     developmental psychology and design for young children. The team sought to develop
     a basic understanding of what it means to be two and three and how children of
     these ages learn and develop. Two books were particularly helpful: Design for Kids,
     Digital Products for Playing and Learning by Debra Levin Gelman 44 and Designing
     Games for Children, Developmental, Usability, and Design Considerations for
     Making Games for Kids by Carla Fisher.45 Both books offered specific information
     about what children can do at different ages. That information, in turn, helped in
     developing design recommendations that became part of the persona. (See Figure 3.1
     for the persona and corresponding design guidelines.)
            The team interacted with children during several play-test sessions 46 to gather
     more specific information about children in the target age group: What did those
     capabilities the team read in the literature really look like? How do these children’s
     cognitive and motor development impact how they interact with and make sense
     of digital games? What is their level of engagement with different genres of digital
     games? How might they respond to the team’s ideas? These initial play-test sessions
                                    Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 101



set the foundation for the product being developed.
       The team was thinking about designing Math Readiness as an open play
environment, for example, a clubhouse or playground, where children would explore
and visit different areas and play the learning games as they encountered them.
To assess the potential of this idea, in one play session, they observed and probed
as children interacted with a popular commercial digital game called Toca Life.47
Toca Life (https://tocaboca.com) is an open-ended game where children make up
stories as they explore designed village-type worlds. As team members observed,
they looked for answers to a set of specific behavioral questions: How do children
navigate around the village and in the different buildings? What help do they need
to learn to navigate? How well do they navigate within buildings? How capable are
they of dragging on the screen? How else do they interact with items? What do they
enjoy doing?
       The team play-tested with five children ages two and three and found that all
were very engaged with Toca Life and most (four out of five) enjoyed and were able
to drag objects and characters around. Some engaged in more advanced role-play
mechanics of the game such as cooking, using some game items, and interacting
with others. Based on this, they felt comfortable designing a game built around an
open play environment. The children’s interaction with Toca Life also suggested to
the team that they would have to design such that the learning games would attract
the children and keep their attention, as in the open world of Toca Life, the children
navigated around the village sometimes in seemingly random ways. They learned
also that young children tended to drag with two fingers, which led the team to
decide they needed to design the set of games with multi-touch drag.
       Based on reading and observations, the team was now ready to develop a
persona, that is, a model with enough information in it about a child interacting with
a math readiness game that they could use it to predict the way two- and three-year-
olds would interact with different functions in the game they were designing. They
named their initial persona Lily. Her name and the photo in the persona made her
real to the team. The persona includes the amount of exposure to technology Lily has
had, her “catchphrase,” and details about her family. Knowing that Lily (the typical
two- or three-year-old they are designing for) has parents or grandparents available
to her suggests the need to consider that Lily might play alone when her parents give
her the iPad to keep her occupied and that she might play along with grandma (or
other relatives) at other times.
102 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



                                                          Design Guidelines
                                                          (1) Lily understands and enjoys
                                                          making choices but be mindful when
                                                          asking Lily to choose between two
                                                          items.
                                                          If you ask Lily an either-or question such
                                                          as, Lily, would you like cake OR broccoli?
                                                          she will likely say (85% of the time!) that
                                                          she likes the second item (or the item
                                                          most recently o!ered) due to recency
                                                          bias. However, if you ask Lily if she would
                                                          like cake or broccoli and have her point
                                                          to which one she wants, she will choose
Persona Development: Lily                                 the first or second item roughly the same
                                                          amount of times.48
!"#"$%&
• Lily is two years old                                   (2) When using visual communication
• Speaks English at home
                                                          with Lily, be as literal as possible.
• Doesn’t go to school yet
                                                          Lily doesn’t understand the abstract yet.

• Parents are married; both work
                                                          highly representative of the task we are
• Grandparents actively involved in upbringing

• Spends time with parents and at daycare
• No siblings                                             Lily cannot read, so pictures and icons are
                                                          even more important. If we need more than
+%,-./.$%0"
