• “Mine!”
                                                          is supposed to work, the interaction is too
1"-.234/506$"                                             complicated for her.50
• Mom has an iPhone 11
• Dad has an iPhone 11                                    (3) Provide moments for surprise and
• Family has an iPad Air used for
                                                          delight throughout the activity.
  fun and to occupy Lily
• Lily’s parents are less likely to purchase              Lily loves cause and e!ect. Lily gets a kick
  games that she might get bored of quickly,              out of tapping something and watching or
  so she mainly spends time watching videos               hearing it respond.
                                                          She also really likes it when something fun
                                                          and unexpected happens.51

       (4) Because Lily’s fine motor skills are not yet fully developed, object drop
       zones and tap zones for Lily should be large.
       Lily can drag but is liable to miss her target or experience other difficulties. She is better at
       tapping.
       She tends to use multiple fingers or her whole hand to move objects on the screen or to
                                         Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 103



scroll. When possible, make use of full hand gestures instead of relying on the thumb and
index finger alone.52 Her dragging will improve with practice.

Lily still struggles with small, fine gestures since her fine motor skills are not yet fully
developed. She needs big on-screen elements to manipulate. Smaller ones are still difficult
for her to interact with.53
Lily thinks of “pinching” as picking up a small object or something you do to a friend when
you’re mad at them, not as a way to shrink things on a screen.54

(5) Interactive elements should be as prominent as possible, and non-inter-
active backgrounds should not be more visually stimulating than interactive
elements.
Lily cannot yet distinguish main elements of an interface from the details.55 If something
catches her eye, she will focus on it until she becomes frustrated that it doesn’t do anything.
Lily can only see items on screen in two dimensions, not three. Meaning, everything on a
screen looks like it’s in a single, flat plane to her. If we want to use foreground elements,
they should be much clearer and more detailed than anything in the background.56
Lily will get overwhelmed when there are too many items competing for her attention. We
should pick the smallest possible set of easily identifiable elements (like colors) and use
them consistently throughout her experiences.57

(6) Allow Lily to get to the interactions as soon as possible.
Lily is impulsive, acting quickly rather than thinking things through. (She’s still working on
executive function.)58
It is critical for Lily to experiment to learn.59 Experimentation takes many forms: she wants
to try stacking blocks in di!erent ways, test boundaries like seeing how far she can be from
her parents before being called back, or making funny sounds over and over.

(7) Lily enjoys and learns through repetition.
Lily is willing to repeat practice tasks she finds enjoyable while working toward mastery.60

(8) Draw extra attention to any numbers said via voiceover instructions.
We want Lily to think about numbers and hear them as often as possible in everyday
language to accelerate her numeracy development.61




FIGURE 3.1. Initial persona of a two-year-old, which evolved with more detailed and
accurate information as we tested our design ideas. Also included is a summary of
how the Lily persona might respond to cues and therefore how to design for her.
104 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



            Lily’s parents might decide what she will play with, while her grandma may
     give her more choices. These entries in the persona captured variations in the con-
     texts in which children might interact with the game and suggested the need to design
     parent (or caretaker) materials. Figure 3.1 shows the remainder of the persona too,
     such as the different ways Lily might respond to cues and therefore how to design
     for her. It considers that children like Lily are new to the digital world, just learning
     how to interact with touch screens, and developing fine motor skills, attention span,
     and listening skills. Another developmental feature of two- and three-year-olds is
     that they can respond to the same thing in myriad ways; except for that key phrase—
     Mine!—there’s a lot of variation in a single child. Thus, creating a single persona
     with many possibilities was both an accurate way to model an individual child and a
     way to capture variations across different two- and three-year-olds.62
            The information in the persona would be used throughout the rest of the de-
     velopment process to answer questions about what Lily would do or how she would
     react when interacting with suggested game mechanics. It includes a range of be-
     haviors and design suggestions and warnings, covering a large swath of the two- and
     three-year-old population. When the persona could not provide answers to questions
     the team had while designing, they knew they needed to find answers through addi-
     tional observation or testing. As they critiqued and tested their design ideas in later
     iterations, the initial persona evolved to become more detailed and accurate.


     ITERATIVE DESIGN PHASE

     The team had generated game ideas during the exploratory phase, and now they
     began focusing on those ideas: critiquing them, refining them, combining them, and
     generating more. Because Lily enjoys exploring an open play environment, they de-
     cided to organize the learning activities in Math Readiness within an environment
     that Lily can explore. Because Lily enjoys activities where she can make something
     happen, they decided to design a series of highly interactive games, re-playable mu-
     sic videos, and interactive stories. Because their goal is to prepare Lily to learn
     pre-K math concepts and skills, these activities would introduce her to counting,
     tracing numerals, and recognizing and creating patterns. And because it’s not pos-
     sible to predict when Lily will be ready for pre-K math, they decided to include
     activities that they could use to judge her understanding and allow the game to rec-
     ommend her readiness to graduate to My Math Academy. The rest of this case study
     focuses on a single game in Math Readiness called Pattern Pathway. It’s designed to
     help children learn to complete and recognize simple patterns. Because Lily is still
                                    Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 105



developing her motor skills, the team needed to find out more detail about the game
mechanics Lily could be expected to manage. They built a prototype and play-test-
ed it with additional two- and three-year-olds. In addition to assessing their motor
skills, the team wanted to know more about their enjoyment of dragging game com-
ponents across the screen; they were imagining a game where children would drag
game items to make patterns.


FIRST TESTS OF THE PATTERN PATHWAY GAME

Play-testing was to be carried out in three rounds. In the first two, children would be
asked to complete AB patterns, which included only two different items that alter-
nated with each other, like ABABAB. In the third round, children would complete
an ABC pattern (like ABCABCABC . . . ). For the third round, the hit box, which
is the part of an object that can be used for dragging, was smaller than in the first
two rounds. The designers wanted to know how big hit boxes would need to be for
successful dragging and dropping. They also included three additional questions:
      • How well are these children understanding the game-play mechanics?
      • What does it take to get them to understand those mechanics?
      • How much do they enjoy the stories that go with each game?
    The moderator told each child that they’re going to play a game about Shapey
who must go over a bridge and that they’ll help Shapey. (See Figure 3.2.) Then




FIGURE 3.2. Initial prototype of the Pattern Pathway game
106 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




     FIGURE 3.3. Paper prototype of the Pattern Pathway game used in the third test


     children listened to Shapey read the pattern aloud (“red, blue, red . . . ”) and were
     asked to choose the next block to help Shapey get across to the other side. They
     play-tested with six two- to three-year-olds and learned that five out of six were able
     to drag a block (correctly or incorrectly) to the bridge, indicating that dragging is an
     interaction that would work for this age group and this concept. They also found that
     asking the children to help Shapey cross the bridge was simple enough for children
     to understand and was enjoyable for most. There was one child who had some diffi-
     culty dragging the smaller blocks in round three (ABC pattern), which led to the de-
     cision to keep the hit box as large as it was for rounds one and two in all of the games.


     SECOND TEST OF THE PATTERN PATHWAY GAME

     At this point, the team decided to use a playground as the open play environment.
     Children would go to different areas in the playground to play different learning
     games. In the earlier session with Pattern Pathway, they had play-tested with a
     digital prototype, but at this time in the production process, they didn’t have the
     resources to update the digital build with the new playground theme. Instead, they
     built a paper prototype of this new game. (See Figure 3.3.)
            In this test session, they wanted to check what kinds of instructions and visuals
                                     Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 107



would provide clear guidance so children would know what to do to play the game.
(Remember that Lily might not always have an adult with her while playing.) They
play-tested with another six children, one at a time with an adult moderator. Three of
the children were two-year-olds and three were three-year-olds. They hypothesized
that two-year-olds would need more guidance to learn this activity than three-year-
olds, and interactions between the adult moderator and each child would allow them
to figure out what kinds of audio instructions and online cues would be needed. The
moderator asked the two- and three-year-olds to drag step stools to the empty slots;
the question was which game instructions resulted in the children recognizing what
they should do.
       They found that the playground was a fun setting for the children. They were
engaged and enjoyed creating their own narratives using the game pieces. However,
even though the playground was in the background and not in the foreground,
the children were more interested in making Shapey interact with objects on the
playground, for example, taking him down the slide or having him play in the
sandbox, than completing the pattern as they were asked to do. None of the two-
year-olds followed the intentions of the game to complete the pattern. Two of the
three-year-olds attended to the pattern, but only one of the three-year-olds replicated
it. The team didn’t know whether the activity they created was a poor one for these
children, if the activity might have had better results if it had been built digitally, if
the background called too much attention to itself, or if the time lag (between getting
the physical pieces of the game ready and the children actually interacting with the
game) was the problem. Using a digital artifact, the children would have been able
to interact with the game immediately after turning it on. In a digital game, also,
the creators can design what parts are interactable and / or use animation to draw
children’s attention to the game itself.


THIRD TEST OF THE PATTERN PATHWAY GAME

The team developed a digital version of the same game (Figure 3.4) and tested it
with another six children. Would the digital version of the game better grab their
attention? To invite the attention of the children to the game, the screen was designed
with a brighter foreground than background. The playground area in the background
was smaller and had less playground equipment in it. And only objects designed for
the game itself were interactive. When the children dragged the stepping stools into
place, Shapey hopped to the next one. In addition, there was a “helping hand” on the
screen that modeled how to drag a stepping stool.
108 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




     FIGURE 3.4. Digital version of the same game used in the third test


           As expected, children’s interactions with the game were much more focused
     on the game itself: dragging the stepping stools to complete the pattern. All of
     the children attended to the animated objects, and most weren’t distracted by the
     non-interactive elements in the background. The helping hand, however, was not
     something the children understood. The moderator needed to prompt most of the
     children to show them that the hand could show how to drag the blocks. A couple
     of children tried tapping the helping hand. Several children also exhibited “happy
     tapping,” where they rapidly tapped the stool with Shapey on it or the one they
     needed to drag. One child clicked the slide in the background, and one child tapped
     the screen with their entire hand. Most children exhibited joy while playing the
     game, and none tried to exit the game before completion.
           The team made modifications to the game, including (a) changing the
     instructions to make the objective clearer, (b) making game-play mechanics more
     forgiving and allowing children to drag the stools short distances several times to get
     them to where they needed to go, and (c) adjusting the art assets, visual drop zone,
     and animations to help communicate to children where to place the blocks and to
     help them understand the narrative. A screenshot of the next version of the game is
     shown in Figure 3.5.
                                   Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 109




FIGURE 3.5. Final version of the Pattern Pathway game


FINAL TESTING

The next round of play-testing was done with another seven children. The team
found that with their modifications to enable more flexibility in core game-play
mechanics (such as tapping, dragging, swiping), children could easily place blocks
in the path. All children successfully interacted with blocks and dragged them to the
correct drop zones. All completed the pattern on the pathway, though one needed
moderator prompting on the last piece to complete the entire pattern. Children
could interact with the first block and make patterns with minimal or no moderator
support. The interface was simple enough for the children, and the on-screen visuals
were arranged so that children could identify what they could interact with and
were not distracted by non-interactive elements in the game. While children were
not distracted by the background, one did tap around the screen briefly early on, but
once core game play started, he focused on completing the pattern. The children
all exhibited joy and excitement while playing the game. Some children giggled
after dragging the block to the correct location, and most looked to their parents,
apparently proud of having done it successfully.
110 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     PREPARING FOR FINAL RELEASE

     Once validated, the team moved forward to prepare the game for release. Mean-
     while, insights gathered from the design process for Pattern Pathways were added to
     the company’s compilation of research-based design guidelines for informing future
     designs for two- and three-year-olds (Chapter 2). Pattern Pathways and nine other
     Math Readiness games are now available for two- and three-year-olds as a precursor
     to My Math Academy. The team moved on to collect interaction data of children
     playing this and other Math Readiness games, and they are analyzing them to inform
     the next set of improvements.


                                            !&"
     The two cases presented below show steps in designing digital interventions for the
     health sector in Africa. Both demonstrate how participatory design that included
     members of the targeted community of learners contributed to a good final product.
     Both also show different ways members of the targeted community of learners can
     be integrated into design teams and the roles they can play. The first case study is
     of developing a game on HIV education for a stigmatized population who find it
     difficult to trust those outside their community. An iterative participatory approach
     that included a series of meetings created trust and a safe space. The designers
     listened to members of the stigmatized population, their ideas were appreciated,
     and they felt safe to openly contribute sensitive concepts that designers then used
     to make the game engaging and relatable. The second case shows how pictograms
     on design cards enabled health workers who might not have had the confidence,
     language, vocabulary, or literacy to articulate their needs or thoughts to the app
     developers. These cards enabled participation across languages, cultures, and social
     and power structures.


     Leti Arts’ KNO, for Those at Risk for HIV
     This case study describes the design process for an interactive role-playing game
     that was developed in Ghana to encourage behavior change among a population
     that was engaging in risky sexual behavior. Key populations—people who inject
     drugs, men who have sex with men (MSM), transgender persons, sex workers, and
     prisoners—experience major barriers to accessing HIV prevention, treatment, and
     other health services. The challenge was to develop tailored and integrated strategies
                                    Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 111



to educate these populations about positive living and accessing HIV prevention,
care, and treatment services.
       The national prevalence of HIV in adults fifteen to forty-nine years old in
Ghana is 1.7 percent. Among key populations, the HIV prevalence is significantly
higher. For sex workers, it’s 7.0 percent, and for MSM, it’s more than twice that at
18 percent.63 Stigma, discrimination, marginalization, and low levels of HIV knowl-
edge are determinants of key populations’ vulnerability to HIV.
       An international development project in Ghana was implementing several
outreach strategies to overcome these barriers to HIV education and care.64 A role-
playing game was an approach they used to address one key population, MSM. As in
role-play, they would have opportunities to make choices in scenarios that replicated
what they would normally encounter in real life, experience the outcomes of those
choices as game players, and to get to success in the game, come to make decisions
that would keep them healthier and get them the services they needed.
       The project funded Leti Arts to develop the game. The aim of the role-playing
game was to promote safe sex, decrease alcohol consumption, and encourage
counseling and testing. It was named KNO. With such a generic name, they thought,
nobody would find any bias in the name, and nobody for whom it wasn’t intended
would guess what it was about.
       The game provides opportunities for players to take on the role of a character,
Kaycey, as he engages in different social scenarios. Users make decisions about be-
haviors during a night out and consider how to fix mistakes Kaycey makes following
a night of overindulgent partying. They’re told, “Challenges abound in finding help
with his sexual health decisions, but he has no choice but to man up!”


THE PARTICIPATORY DESIGN TEAM: IT TAKES A VILLAGE

Leti Arts is a game design company based in Ghana. They have been building games
for both entertainment and education since 2009. The design team included Leti
Arts staff, a liaison, and experts from the international development organization
that contracted with Leti Arts, representatives of community health organizations,
peer educators, government officials, and, most important of all, members of the
MSM population the game was targeted for. The HIV counselors, peer educators,
members of the targeted group, medical professionals, and others from the different
organizations acted as subject-matter experts. The Leti Arts team members man-
aged the project and developed the app.
       The Leti Arts team included a project manager, a creative director, and
112 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     programmers, developers, illustrators, and sound experts. The project manager man-
     aged the relationship with the international development project that was funding the
     game and coordinated the tasks to ensure expectations were met and deliverables
     were being completed on time. The creative director led the design of the app. Con-
     tent creators worked with subject-matter experts to design the interactive scenarios,
     storyboards, and learning experiences; prepare the scripts for narration; and lay out
     the interactions and flow. The programmers, developers, and UI / UX designers built
     the front end, back end, and game design. Leti Arts has developed gaming software
     and stays current with the latest gaming applications. Working together, the mem-
     bers of the participatory design team developed role-play scenarios, characters, user
     interactions, and alternative experiences of users based on their choices.


     THE FIRST CONTENT DEVELOPMENT MEETING

     The first workshop was held in the capital of Ghana, Accra. The Leti Arts staff
     helped others understand the process of game design and the idea of a role-playing
     game. They guided the team as they identified scenarios for the game. Two ideas
     emerged for game scenarios that would cover all the different topics.

        ► A Safe Night Out. Kaycey goes out for a night on the town; players interact
           with the game to make a series of decisions about his behavior. Depending on
           their choices, they have different experiences and get to make other decisions.
           In this way, they “experience” the effects of decisions around sex and alcohol
           use. As they play the game over and over, they get to make better decisions
           and experience their effects.
        ► The Morning After . . . In this scenario, players role-playing Kaycey consider
           the effects of the choices he made the night before and act out decision-making
           about his sexual health, deciding on the best course of action to ensure he stays
           safe.

            After coming up with the two scenarios, the design team continued by
     creating user interactions and game design flow for each scenario. As they worked,
     the artist was continuously making sketches based on what he was hearing, sharing
     his illustrations, and asking for feedback.
            Between meetings, Leti Arts staff refined the illustrations and worked on nar-
     rations and use of other media, laying out potential routes through the game flow,
     and the technical details underlying those. Sometimes some of the staff made site
                                       Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 113



visits. One site visit was to restaurants, bars, and other venues members of the MSM
community frequent. They sought to learn more about those venues and meet addi-
tional members of the MSM community. Another site visit was to health facilities
where they interviewed health workers to learn about interactions during counseling
sessions. At the next participatory design team meetings, new graphics, versions of
the storyline, and insights were shared. Each new design and insight were opportu-
nities to make the app more appealing and worthwhile to the target population.
       Over time, the subject-matter experts and Leti Arts staff became more com-
fortable with one another. As trust and understanding were established within the
team, new layers of content emerged, and the complexity of what needed to be de-
veloped became more evident.


THE THIRD CONTENT DEVELOPMENT MEETING

In the third content-development session, the members of the MSM population became
quite engaged and excited after seeing the artist’s illustrations and listening to the
storyline. They provided more depth and nuance about the people and interactions
within their community, and the game became more real to them. Discussions
focused on the names of characters, their backgrounds and professions, the clothes
the characters should be wearing, and how they should speak. A suggestion was
made that one set of characters in the out-on-the-town scenario should be dressed
in Lacoste or Polo shirts and have more introverted personalities, and another set
should be dressed in flashier, brighter-colored clothes and be more outspoken and
more likely to try (and encourage) new (and riskier) things. As the drawings became
more real, researchers also learned more about variations across the end users they
were designing for.
       It became clear, as well, during this session, that the initial rounds of illustrations
did not adequately enough represent the characters and scenes. The lead artist made
significant changes to the physiques of the characters after that session, giving them
more defined, shaped, muscular bodies than what he had initially imagined. (See
Figure 3.6.)
       Colors were also discussed and needed to be changed. The initial thematic col-
ors for the app were unappealing to the men from the MSM population. (These are
shown in Figure 3.7 on the left.) They wanted the scenes in the night-out scenario to
be moodier and more suspenseful and use more contrasting colors, more like the de-
cor in the places they frequented and the clothes they wore. Purple was chosen as the
thematic main color, and illustrations were adapted and made consistent throughout
114 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                                                              FIGURE 3.6. Changes were
                                                              made to the initial design of
                                                              the character after feedback
                                                              from the targeted population




     the app. Figure 3.7 (on the right) shows the opening screen of the night-out scenario,
     and Figure 3.8 shows the decor of the jazz club.
            This session also marked a turning point in support from these design-group
     participants. They now really wanted Kaycey to succeed in changing his behaviors.
     They provided additional feedback and insight on the tasks, interactions, and deci-
     sions in the game, offering more detailed and real-life advice about the types of de-
     cisions people would make in the different scenarios, for example, how men engage
     with the men in a bar, who they go home with, using condoms, and more.


     DESIGNING WITH THE USER IN MIND

     The Leti Arts team went through their own journey of understanding the MSM
     community. In the beginning, this work was based on their preconceived percep-
     tions about the community. As they learned more about the community and began




     FIGURE 3.7. Two possible menu screens were considered for when end users open
     the game. Following design considerations, the initial screen (left) was replaced with a
     more vibrant screen (right).
                                     Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 115



to place themselves in the characters, they
shifted their perspective and how the case
studies evolved. The storyline, characters,
and language used for the narratives and
visualizations were refined through each
iteration.
       The game designers knew that the
game needed a constant balance between
learning and play to hold players’ attention.     FIGURE 3.8. Complementary colors
How could the story be told so that it is         were used in the jazz club
engaging and entertaining while not boring
and still educational? In the end, the scenarios do not overtly suggest to the MSMs
that the app is educating them, but rather, it does so subtly, through narrations, tasks,
and decision points. Members of the MSM community offered their expertise as real
team members, while Leti Arts experts integrated game mechanics and gamification
techniques (points and rewards) to motivate game players and showcase their
emerging expertise in engaging ways.


TESTING THE GAME AND ROLLING IT OUT

The game was tested in two locations: in Accra, the capital, and Kumasi, a region
north of Accra. It was tested with representatives from two populations: peer
educators and potential users. Testing with peer educators resulted in the Leti Arts
team refining the night-out story to incorporate a stronger focus on alcohol abuse.
Dialogue and decisions were added on how much alcohol to drink and if Kaycey
should accept or decline offers from those offering to buy him drinks. Testing with
potential users led to additional dialogue refinement.
       After incorporating additional feedback from the testing, the game was ready
for distribution. To maintain the privacy and security of users, and to make sure they
felt safe playing the game, it was never made available to the public. Rather, it was
distributed by peer educators. Each peer educator was given an access code to share
with the users they are in contact with. New users were invited in by existing users
through SMS or email; they were added to a white-list database, created profiles,
and were given access to the game. The access screen has never depicted anything
that indicates the nature of the game. The data collected included logs tracking dis-
tribution and use of the game. The participatory design approach led to a game that
is relatable and engaging to members of the key population.
116 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Medic Mobile’s Community Health App
     How do you encourage participation in the design process when people who need to be
     members of the design team together speak different languages, come from different
     cultures, have different perspectives, may lack the confidence to speak, or may be
     illiterate? Medic Mobile, a nonprofit organization that builds and deploys digital tools
     to improve health equity worldwide, has developed an approach that brings together
     relevant people across the health sector, including community members, nurses, and
     managers who speak different languages, are in hard-to-reach settings, and who
     don’t have internet connectivity. Medic Mobile uses a human-centered participatory
     design approach to guide use cases, impact workflow design, and build technology
     road maps. They’ve also developed a set of cards with drawings as an integral tool
     in their approach. Participatory design teams use the cards to communicate as they
     develop use cases, work out expected impact, and lay out workflows and technology
     road maps as shown in Figure 3.9.65
             Medic Mobile relies on input from people involved in health care delivery at
     every stage of design and implementation as they identify opportunities and ideate
     health care solutions. Most of their projects focus on health care provider workflows,
     which are complex and remarkably diverse from place to place. Medic Mobile staff
     can sketch workflows, but sketching takes skills that many participants on their
     participatory design teams don’t have. (See Figure 3.10.)
             To assist with the process, their custom-made design cards break down the
     most common elements in health system workflows. This allows even those who
     have never articulated workflows before to make sketches of their workflows quickly
     and easily. Each card has a drawing of a different component of the health care
     system: community members, health care workers, places, technology, and actions




   DEFINE                    IDEATE                  DEPLOY                 MEASURE




     FIGURE 3.9. Medic Mobile’s human-centered approach: define, ideate, deploy, measure
                                               Learning Engineering is Human-Centered | 117



                                     REGISTRATION



                                                        ERS
                                                     ND
                                                   MI
                                                RE
                                                                           ON
                                                                       ATI
                                                                    IRM
                                                               O NF
                                                           IT C RT
                                                        VIS REPO
             REFERRAL
                                                                                    IF NO VISIT
                                                                                    CONFIRMATION
                                    FOLLOW-UP
                                                                                    CHEW IS
                                                                                    NOTIFIED

                                                VISIT ATTENDANCE


FIGURE 3.10. Sketch of an SMS-enabled antenatal care intervention
In this example mock-up of the workflow or mechanism of action for antenatal care (ANC) intervention, (1) a
CHW registers a pregnant woman via SMS; (2) software installed at a hospital automatically creates a schedule
of appropriately spaced ANC visits and sends the CHW personalized notifications before each appointment; (3)
the CHW revisits the household to refer the woman for ANC; (4) typically the pregnant woman visits the clinic;
(5) the CHW follows up a few days later and (6) sends an SMS to confirm that the appointment was attended. If
no SMS confirmation is received, the CHW’s manager (the CHEW) is automatically notified. Partners often find
such workflow sketches more participatory and accessible to input (especially across language, culture, and
power barriers) than technical product specifications or detailed written/verbal descriptions alone. New Projects
involve many variations of such sketches as designs for technology and service delivery co-evolve.


involved—such as visits to clinics. These cards are a visual way to engage people in
role-play to guide the articulation process, help facilitate understanding of the work,
and imagine new ways of doing it. For example, the pictures in Figure 3.11 tell stories
about the medicine supply chain (part a), how a community health worker gets to the
community to visit pregnant women and mothers to check how they are doing (part
b), and options for how data are transferred to a ministry of health (part c).
      The cards invite everyone from community health workers to scientists and
policy makers to be active participants in workshops. Anybody can point to or re-
arrange the cards as they speak to explain environments, goals, assets, and pain
points. This experience helps to create a feeling that they are all creating something
together. This is particularly important when working across language barriers. The
design cards take some responsibility from the translators and focus the conversation
on the system being laid out on the table. The teams Medic Mobile puts together in
118 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



                                                              FIGURE 3.11. Medic
                                                              Mobile’s story cards

                                                              TOP PANEL: What does the
!+"                                                           process look like to deliver and
                                                              track supplies to and at the health
                                                              facility or hospital for the nurse
                                                              to administer the medicines?

                                                              MIDDLE PANEL: The community
                                                              health organization is planning a
                                                              visit to the community to check
                                                              on pregnant women and new
                                                              mothers. What transport is available
!,"                                                           to take her to the location? What
                                                              information will she collect?

                                                              BOTTOM PANEL: What technology is
                                                              available (feature phone or laptop)?
                                                              What are the options to transfer data
                                                              to the ministry of health depending
                                                              on access to the internet (send to the
                                                              cloud or collect off-line and sync when
!-"                                                           internet access is available)? What
                                                              data do the ministry want? What type
                                                              of charts and graphs are useful?




      designing applications may have people who don’t speak the same language or come
      from different cultures and countries. It could be people in the same country that don’t
      speak the local dialect, Americans or European international development workers
      involved in projects in rural African communities, or designers from one country
      in Africa, such as from Kenya, working with target communities in other African
      countries where the people do not speak Swahili. Design cards give all parties a
      tool to communicate ideas and explore scenarios in a concrete and tangible form.
             Using these cards to make a participatory approach work means that workflows
      and tools being developed are grounded in the reality of the context and informed by
      input from health workers on the ground. Designs informed by those who carry out
      workflows are more likely to be easily integrated into daily work practices. These
      practices will ultimately lead to creating an improved health delivery system for
      their communities.
• Learning engineering requires human-centered design.

• Human-centered design starts by understanding the challenge from the
  learners’ perspectives and then creates solutions through research-
  based iterative design.

• Learning engineering’s human-centered perspective came from several
  fields including human-centered design, design thinking, universal design
  for learning, learning experience design, and design-based research.

• Learners are the central component in the system of learning, and they
  should be active participants in their own learning.

• Human-centered design is an iterative process that relies on data and
  data-driven decisions. It doesn’t stop with the conception design; it
  continues as details are added, including implementation details.

• For learning, human-centered design typically involves understanding
  learner variability (for example developing personas to refer back to
  during design), making ideas concrete with prototypes, and testing ideas
  iteratively with representatives of the learner population. The process can
  include the following:
     – Empathy development via observing or interacting with learners
     – Co-design, or participation of learners in the design process
     – Translation of ideas to rapid prototypes, for example, design cards
     – Testing ideas quickly with learners, for instance, to ensure the
       learners are meaningfully engaged, for sustainment of motivation
       and engagement, for feelings of being invited into the process, and
       for moving learning forward
     – Iterative refinement with simple prototypes, which become more
       complex and developed with continued user input
     – Iterative refinement during implementation with continued user input

• The mindset of a learning engineering design team should be that the
  product is the learning experience (or the set of conditions in which
  learning can be successful) rather than the content or technology.



                           LEARNING ENGINEERING IS H UMAN-CENTERED

                    KEY POINTS

CHAPTER 4


Learning Engineering
is Engineering
by Avron Barr, Brandt Dargue, Jim Goodell, and Brandt Redd



Learning engineering is a process and practice that applies
the learning sciences using human-centered engineering
design methodologies and data-informed decision-
making to support learners and their development.



Without Margaret Hutchinson Rousseau and her team of engineers and scientists,
large-scale production of penicillin wouldn’t have been possible during World War
II. Many more lives would have been lost, and it might have impacted the outcome
of the war—and the world as we know it today.
       Margaret was a Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) trained chemical
engineer. This chapter features stories from people trained as engineers and who
applied its general principles to learning engineering. Bror Saxberg was trained as
an electrical engineer (at MIT) and medical doctor (at Harvard University) and later
became a learning engineer. Piotr Mitros was trained as an electrical engineer and
later became a learning engineer. He also developed the MITx platform, which is
now Open edX.
       Avron Barr, a co-author of this chapter, is a software engineer, expert in
artificial intelligence, and a learning engineer. Co-author, Brandt Redd comes from
a family of engineers and has applied his software engineering background and deep
knowledge of the learning sciences to the field of learning engineering.
       We will explore in this chapter some common principles that apply to any kind
of engineering—mechanical engineering, civil engineering, chemical engineering,
software engineering, and learning engineering.



DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-8
126 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                                              !!"
     Bror Saxberg knows a lot about the science of learning and the practice of learning
     engineering. His medical doctorate from Harvard Medical School and doctorate of
     philosophy in electrical engineering and computer science from MIT give him a
     unique background and credibility. After proving to be an expert learner, he took
     on the personal mission to help others learn at scale. Before his doctorates at MIT
     and Harvard, he was a Rhodes scholar, earning a master’s degree in mathemat-
     ics at Oxford University. But his career isn’t limited to academic achievements. He
     has brought his expertise into real-world situations, promoted learning engineering
     practices, and impacted hundreds of thousands of students.
            Bror’s stature and light complexion reflect his Nordic heritage. His father Borje
     “Bud” Saxberg emigrated to the US from Finland on a scholarship from Oregon
     State University. Bud Saxberg joined the faculty of University of Washington in
     1957 after marrying his wife Margaret, a Norwegian student, and after the birth of
     Bror.1 Bror’s father and mother encouraged him to gain a deeper understanding of
     mathematics. As a result, Bror now says “deep exposure to mathematics gave me an
     enormous toolkit to engage and understand the principles and problem-solving of
     engineering.” 2 Additionally, his father’s career as a teacher helped inspire his inter-
     est in teaching and learning.
            After doing research in cognitive science related to human and machine vision
     at MIT’s Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, Bror learned about putting people, ideas,
     technology, and finances together into successful organizations while at manage-
     ment consulting firm McKinsey and Company. Later, he applied his knowledge as
     Chief Academic Officer at K12, Inc., a company that provides online curricula to
     virtual charter schools, home-school parents, and schools that want to supplement
     course offerings, and then as Chief Learning Officer at Kaplan University 3 (now
     Purdue University Global, an accredited online college serving 28,000 students with
     175 degree-programs). He was also general manager for the multimedia division of
     DK Publishing.
            More recently, as vice president of learning science at the Chan Zuckerberg
     Initiative, his career moved from cognitive scientist to learning engineer, to one who
     advances the practice of learning engineering at scale, prompting him to remark:

           I rely on scientific research about learning and minds, but I use it to
           solve real problems. I am, essentially, a learning engineer. 4
                                          Learning Engineering is Engineering | 127



       Bror is articulate, precise, and energetic when discussing learning engineer-
ing. His face lights up when talking about how learning works and about discoveries
that lead to better learning.
       He acknowledges that while learning engineering builds on the science of
learning, it’s not limited to blindly applying existing scientific discoveries. As with
any engineering task, we should ask, What’s the science behind this? How do we
use that science to make robust learning environments? And beyond that, we should
look to new experimentation, the collection and analysis of data to understand weak-
nesses in the current approach in context and for specific learners, and to test the
effectiveness of alternative solutions. Engineering also involves making trade-offs
and navigating real-world constraints to achieve practical outcomes. For example,
“‘how do we measure learning progress over short and long time frames?’ Education
[and training] cannot be defined by a few numbers; still, we shouldn’t shy away from
objective information to improve the student experience.” 5 We’ll examine more of
Bror’s story in Chapter 6.


                                        !""
In March 2012 MIT launched the MITx platform, the predecessor to edX, the
massive open online course (MOOC) platform created by Harvard and MIT that
now hosts over 3,000 university-level courses offered by 150 schools, nonprofit or-
ganizations, and corporations. These courses are taken by about thirty-three million
students worldwide. Some courses are offered at no charge. edX runs on the free
edX open source software platform. The global nonprofit, edX is transforming tra-
ditional education, removing the barriers of cost, location, and access. The story of
the Open edX platform is a good illustration of engineering mindsets and methods
applied to a learning solution.
       Piotr Mitros began building MITx in mid-November 2011, and the first course
on it was launched to MIT students less than three months later, in early February
2012. By the beginning of March, the course was available to the world. The coders
had a rapid development cycle, in part because Piotr had been working on the con-
cepts behind MITx (and Open edX) for many years.
       In 2007, as a graduate student at MIT, he worked on a project with Obafemi
Awolowo University (OAU), based in Nigeria. It had thirty-thousand students. The
electrical engineering department had seven-hundred students, and about a dozen
instructors, of whom three were full faculty.
       Even though the faculty were hard working and highly qualified, the
128 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     teacher–student ratios severely limited the traditional education model. Piotr ap-
     plied an engineering mindset to the problem. He came up with models that blended
     technology, peer learning, and human instruction. He wasn’t able to implement these
     at OAU, but they became the basis of the MITx project and eventually the Open edX
     platform.
             In 2007, Piotr discussed this with Rafael Reif, the current president of MIT
     who was the school’s provost then. “At the time, I was a graduate student, with very
     little savings, and no credibility.” It would take a few more years before a project
     would be funded. The time wasn’t wasted, however. Piotr became an early employee
     at a medical imaging startup. There he developed the analog electronics for a new
     medical instrument that can remotely image the surface of the heart, shaving hours
     off of a particular type of atrial surgery and dramatically increasing its accuracy. It’s
     been used on thousands of patients, saving many lives.
             When he built the MITx platform, Piotr had a lot of practical experience
     teaching and training as an engineer but admits he wasn’t an experienced learning
                                       scientist or learning engineer. “Fortunately, I had
                                       support from people who were such experts.” The
 LE AR NIN  G  EN  GIN  EE  RIN G      learning gains achieved on the MITx platform would
  IS OF TE  N  A TE AM    SPO  RT      have been impossible without the contributions of
    IN WH ICH     TH E  LE  AD         others. Piotr spoke to as many experts as he could
 DE VELOP ERS DE PE ND ON              and integrated the most promising concepts into
       OTH ER EX PE RTS                a coherent system. Many of these concepts came
                                       from his thesis advisor, Jerry Sussman (a student of
     SEE CHAPTER 10,                   Seymour Papert’s and a renowned computer science
   TOOLS FOR TEAMING                   educator), David Prichard (a top physics education
                                       researcher with a deep understanding of the science
                                       of how people learn), David’s graduate students
     (especially Daniel Seaton), and Chris Terman (one of the highest-regarded lecturers
     at MIT). In addition, as he had helped Sebastian Thrun launch the original AI
     course that led to his founding of Udacity, Piotr stood on his shoulders and on those
     Sebastian learned from, including Sal Khan and Peter Norvig.
             Critically, this group helped him sort truth from fiction—what we know works
     from what we don’t yet know—which is a key process for any learning engineer and
     one that he wasn’t yet qualified to engage in, as it’s a skill that takes years of careful
     work to develop.
             Engineers need focus to solve complex technical problems like, in the case
     of learning engineering, How can we optimize student learning, engagement, and
                                           Learning Engineering is Engineering | 129



growth? A team approach recognizes the value of others who focus on project man-
agement, human resources, implementation, and other factors of success, allowing
learning engineers to focus on engineering.
       By integrating expert input and prior work, Piotr’s engineering goal for the
design of Open edX was to build an engaging, pedagogically effective platform as
quickly as possible.
       Cost is central to engineering. The design process was to take what was
known about how people learn and come up with simple ways to implement those
techniques in a learning platform where content could be authored at reasonable
cost. He estimates that the cost of content and platform development for the original
Open edX platform and course were roughly a half-million dollars. Jerry Sussman
and Piotr worked more than full time. Chris Terman worked half-time. Anant Agar-
wal probably ramped up to about quarter-time over the course of the project. In
addition, teaching assistants, consultants, and others helped.
       Piotr and the team worked to understand the challenge by sorting through con-
cepts by return on investment, with a large amount of thought on how to implement
effective techniques with low investment into either platform or course development.
The set of techniques he converged on for the original platform were:

      •   Moving from passive to active and then to constructive learning
      •   Complex multi-concept problems
      •   Continuous formative assessment
      •   Remediations for knowledge gaps and misconceptions
      •   Immediate feedback
      •   Mastery learning
      •   Self-paced learning

       This design focused on a limited number of proven learning approaches,
known to be effective for the kinds of courses originally intended for the platform.
Note how this approach contrasts with traditional scientific research that tends to
push into new areas, either researching new interventions or going into further depth
around one intervention. In contrast, good learning engineering uses a broad array
of robust, established techniques based on previous research with well-replicated
results. Engineers also may use data differently than research scientists. For some
scientific studies, outlier data are filtered out as “noise” so that generalized findings
may be discovered. Engineers are more concerned with outlier data, the specific
conditions in which a system tends to fail.
       The scaling of cost is important to learning engineering as well. For example,
130 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     consider how these costs scale:

           • $1 in per-student cost,
           • assuming an average of 1,000 students per course,
           • equals $1,000 cost per course.

           • $1,000 in per-course delivery cost,
           • assuming 1,000 courses on a platform,
           • equals $1,000,000 for the total run-time platform cost.

           • But $1,000 in platform development or maintenance costs,
           • assuming just 1 platform,
           • is still only $1,000 per platform.

     At scale the per-course delivery costs add up, so finding ways to reduce those incre-
     mental costs was a big part of the engineering challenge. For the Open edX platform,
     with millions of students, thousands of courses, and one platform a lot of attention
     was paid to making course creation both economical and effective, and only some
     attention was paid to the platform development costs.
            In many cases, Piotr took techniques from intelligent tutoring systems or adap-
     tive systems and modified them to minimize course authoring costs while retaining
     the same cognitive processes and benefits. For example, Piotr used a combination of
     techniques that allow students to both navigate and contribute to remediations. His
     approach leveraged the MOOC context to crowdsource content tagging, relying on
     learners’ intelligence and a good user interface design instead of machine intelli-
     gence that might take more time and resources to build.
            For the original iterations of the platform, course authoring expertise was not
     a constraint; as a platform designed for MIT professors, there was an assumption
     that the people engineering courses would be able to program and use sophisticated
     technologies. Learning engineering, like other engineering fields, often requires ex-
     pertise in specialized tools. Mechanical engineers use computer-aided design; soft-
     ware engineers use integrated development environments. In this case, the system
     was engineered to make course authoring efficient for expert users, rather than make
     it easy for anyone to author content. It was possible for the original MITx platform to
     be deployed quickly based on the assumption that these professionals would be able
     to invest their time into learning how to properly construct courses using existing
     software engineering tools. It was assumed that course authors could already use
     professional tools like version control systems, Python, data visualizations, and that
     they could effectively apply the learning sciences. Or, if they couldn’t already, it was
                                           Learning Engineering is Engineering | 131



assumed that they’d learn to do so.
       As pointed out in Chapter 1, understanding the challenge includes understand-
ing the context of a learning engineering team. If this project wasn’t contextually
situated at MIT, the challenge might have been constrained by the need for more
usable course authoring tools. In fact, the edX platform now includes edX Studio, a
tool for developing edX courses without requiring software development expertise.
Even so, learning engineering is a team sport and not all members of the team need
expertise in all of the tools. For example, Anant Agarwal captured videos at home,
which he handed off to editors, who in turn handed them off to a student to integrate
into the course. Learning engineering often depends on the skills of a multidisci-
plinary team.


                                          !#"
Engineering is the application of creativity and science to solve problems, and
learning engineering is the application of the learning sciences to creatively solve
problems for learners and learning. Different engineering domains differ in the
problems to be solved and the science to be applied in solving those problems.
Mechanical and chemical engineering apply sciences such as physics, material
science, and chemistry, whereas learning engineering applies, for example, cognitive,
sociocultural, behavioral, and motivational sciences.
      Each engineering domain is unique. There are, however, some overarching
principles that apply to any kind of engineering. Whether it’s software, civil, or
learning engineering, the application of engineering includes: 6
      •   Addressing specific problems
      •   End users who are intended to benefit from the solution
      •   Testability
      •   Maintainability
      •   Integrity
      •   Well-defined external integration
      •   Ethics
      •   Management

      In general, an engineer should: 7
      • Understand the absolutes, context, and “the possible” of ethical practice
      • Be able to communicate with a purpose targeted to a group or population
      • Be aware of the constraints of public, private and academic practice
132 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           • Be able to apply fundamental science to system applications
           • Be able to execute at all levels of design: problem definition, estimation,
             figure of merit, rules of thumb, and sanity checks
           • Be able to execute total system design for sustainability
           • Be aware of contextual design variables: performance, constraints,
             variation, and process capability
           • Practice through team projects: problem definition, information
             acquisition, data analysis, trade-off analysis, and optimization
            In other words, an engineer must systematically account for all implications
     of the design and the final product built to that design. An engineer must think of
     how the solution will be used and how it will be built—not just what materials are
     used in the architecture but also the processes used in construction. How will it be
     supported and maintained? How could it be misused? To help consider and account
     for both sets of bullets above, the engineering process is systematic and methodical
     with periodic reviews. Knowledge of human nature, trend analysis, and imagination
     also help. This is just as important for learning engineering. In the story above, Piotr
     Mitros’ team had to consider how the learning system design supported instructors,
     teaching assistants, authors, school administrators, information technology admin-
     istrators, and managers—as well as learners. They had to ensure the design was
     applicable for all scales of use, and they had to consider constraints such as privacy
     laws at all levels of use. They had to evaluate risks of data breaches and even cheat-
     ing. Some of that is done through careful, systematic reviews, and some requires
     experimentation and studies.
            One way that engineers manage the complexity of all the unknowns is by
     creating and using models.8 Models may be used as a simpler representation of the
     complex system or to simulate very complex systems in a way that would be difficult
     or costly in the real world. Civil engineers use models of population growth and oth-
     er demographics to ensure solutions are “right sized” for the life of their use. Civil
     engineers, for example, use models of material strength to ensure safety over time,
     even as the population who uses a bridge or road grows. They use models of weather
     as well as models of how materials decay and erode to ensure that their solutions last.
     Some of these models aren’t precise; rather, they’re estimates that usually have a safe
     margin of error that must be understood by the engineer. Some, like models of the
     strength of materials, are as simple as trigonometry tables or even the multiplication
     tables we used when learning math. For many of these models, the engineer must be
     able to extrapolate to a finer level represented in the table.
            Engineering models, such as computational fluid dynamic models are very
                                            Learning Engineering is Engineering | 133



precise, high-fidelity models. They’re used in the aerospace industry to perform tests
and validations on new designs, for example, for wing shapes. These models can
retain the complexities of, and even add more complexities that are difficult or im-
possible to test in the real world. To bring it back to learning engineering, models
of learners can be used to test learning systems or concepts. Piotr Mitros’ team also
used models of learning such as moving from passive to active and then to construc-
tive learning.
       Models are used to describe aspects of a system and mathematical relation-
ships for understanding and analyzing the system. Different models of the same
system may be developed to understand the system from different perspectives. For
example, there could be one model of the edX platform representing learner interac-
tions, another modeling software architecture, another modeling the content used in
a course, and another modeling the data used within the system. (See Chapter 6 for
some interesting stories about how data are used to build models that help optimize
learning, predict learning outcomes, and improve learning engineering products.)
       Engineers have a mindset; they think about problems to be solved as a part of
a system. Systems thinking offers a wide perspective that includes seeing overall
structures, patterns, and cycles in systems, rather than seeing only the separate and
specific events in a system.9 In learning engineering this includes considering the
context in which learning takes place, for example, how the learning environment
impacts a person’s ability to learn. It also includes the learner as a part in the system.
For example, how does the system adapt learning experiences for learners with
widely varying backgrounds and needs? What affordances allow learners to direct
that adaptation? How is a learner’s ability to learn affected by that adaptation?
       Systems of various components add complexity to the overall design. Engineers
manage complexity by thinking of systems as a set of interoperable components
or modules. Modularizing the system allows engineers to divide up the challenge
into less complex subsystems. Modules of a system have design constraints and
tolerances appropriate for the role they play within the overall system. Interfaces
handle the interactions among modules for the system to work as intended. The
system functions when (a) it includes modules to do all of the required functions,
(b) the modules interoperate with properly functioning interfaces, and (c) each
module performs within tolerances (that is, within the permissible limit or limits of
variation).
       Engineers are also concerned with the environment in which the sys-
tem will operate. When designing a system, an engineer may specify the typical
operating conditions under which a component of the system is expected to operate
134 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     and the tolerance or acceptable range in which the component or system is expected
     to function. For example, electronics engineers designing early smartphones might
     have recognized that typical LCD screen components didn’t function when the tem-
     perature was below freezing or above 90° F / 32° C. If the phone was expected to
     work in colder temperatures then the engineers needed to design a way to keep the
     screen from freezing, for example by adding insulation to keep it warmer. If the
     phone was expected to work in warmer temperatures, like in hot climates, then the
     engineers needed to incorporate a cooling module as well as a sensor and an inter-
     face to control when the cooling module turns on or off.
            In trade-off analysis an engineering team makes decisions that balance cost,
     utility, and other factors. An interesting example is energy production using solar
     cells. The more solar energy (sunlight) goes in, the more usable energy you get out.
     So solar cells are most efficient when pointed directly at the sun. You can add more
     solar energy going in through mirrors and lenses, like magnifying glasses, focusing
     the sun directly on the cells. However, heat destroys the cells; they must be kept cool
     for longevity. An engineer of the system must balance everything using trade-off
     analyses. For instance, air conditioning might cool the cells, but how much energy
     does that use?
            Learning engineers also leverage system thinking, trade-off analysis, and
     modular design. They’re also concerned with constraints, tolerances, and operating
     conditions. If the components of a learning solution are designed for one environ-
     ment, and that operating environment changes, the system may need to be re-engi-
     neered to tolerate a different environment. For example, the traditional classroom
     model of instruction wasn’t designed to operate in a global pandemic. The previous
     system had components to address student engagement that failed when the operat-
     ing conditions changed. Even as schools reopened with remote learning and hybrid
     options, student participation levels plummeted. The system components that previ-
     ously supported student participation—physical space, cultural norms, and in public
     K-12 education, truancy laws and bussing—just failed to function during the pan-
     demic. While the system has always failed to effectively engage some students, even
     in districts that were able to ensure devices and connectivity for all students found
     that 20 percent or more of students were not showing up. Just like the engineers of
     the phone with the temperature-sensitive LCD screen, the most innovative colleges,
     school districts, and independent schools designed new modules to supplement or
     replace the parts of their systems that failed under the new operating conditions.
     These learning engineers also developed new sensors and instrumentation to moni-
     tor student engagement within the new remote learning environment.
                                           Learning Engineering is Engineering | 135




                                        !$"
Great teachers, instructional designers, and learning scientists know how important
feedback is to learners as part of the learning process. In Chapter 1, we examined
learning engineering as a process that involves data-informed feedback loops to
iteratively improve solutions for learning. In this chapter on engineering, we can’t
talk about feedback loops without getting into engineering control theory. Conve-
niently, this theory can be adapted to education and training reasonably well as soon
as you get over some vocabulary hurdles.
        Figure 4.1 shows a basic control system. One example might be a cruise control
for your car. In that case, the reference is the speed you want to go, for example, 65
miles per hour. The controller translates that reference to an input value, the throttle
position. Then the system (including the engine, transmission, drive train, tires, and
so on) produces an output, the actual speed of the car.
        In control theory, this is an open-loop control system, meaning that it has no
feedback. To get a predictable output the controller must have a very good mathe-
matical model of the system and the system itself must be very precise. Open-loop
systems are used when the acceptable margin of error is large, for example, for a
cooling fan.
        A car with an open-loop cruise control would slow down as it climbed hills
and speed up when it descended. For more precise control, real cruise controls use
a feedback loop. A sensor detects the output value (the speed of the car) and returns
it to the controller. The controller takes the difference between the feedback and the
reference values and adjusts the input (throttle) accordingly. This is called a closed-
loop control system.
        Mathematically, taking the difference is subtraction so we call this negative
feedback. In contrast, positive feedback sends systems wildly out of control because
it amplifies any error. A familiar example is pointing a microphone at a speaker. The
terrific squeal that you hear is a positive feedback loop.



REFERENCE                              INPUT                             OUTPUT
                    CONTROLLER                           SYSTEM




FIGURE 4.1. An open-loop control system
136 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           So, when talking about control systems, closed loop is better than open loop,
     and negative feedback is good while positive feedback is bad. The precise engineer-
     ing terminology is the opposite of what we might use in casual conversation. Let’s
     now apply this to education.


     A Personalized Learning System
     If we take the closed-control loop diagram in Figure 4.2 and make a few word sub-
     stitutions, we get a personalized learning system as shown in Figure 4.3. The learner
     is a part of a system that can benefit from feedback. Skills and other capabilities are
     an output that can be measured through assessment (sensor), and the controller in
     the system informs decisions to vary instruction, including instruction in the form
     of formative feedback to the learner.
            For a moment, let’s ignore the feedback loop. In an open-loop learning system,
     a set of learning objectives (such as those established through academic standards,
     competency definitions, or skills descriptors) are translated into instruction in the
     form of textbooks, lectures, and exercises. These activities are delivered to learners
     resulting in skills. Conventional schooling is much like this. To handle a class of a
     dozen or more students, all students engage in the same activities. But remember
     that open-loop systems require a very precise system and a large acceptable margin
     of error. Students come to us with different personalities, talents, preferences, and
     backgrounds. The result is a wide margin of error as illustrated by the spectrum of
     grades assigned at the end of a course.
            Good teachers and good curriculum do better than this. They find ways to pro-
     vide feedback as often as possible and frequently adapt their teaching to the needs of




                                           SENSOR
      FEEDBACK

                                           INPUT                             OUTPUT
     REFERENCE           CONTROLLER                          SYSTEM




     FIGURE 4.2. A closed-loop control system
                                          Learning Engineering is Engineering | 137




                                   ASSESSMENT
 FEEDBACK

                                      INPUT                               SKILLS
STANDARDS           INSTRUCTION                        STUDENT




FIGURE 4.3. Control theory for learning, a personalized learning system

the class or individual students. Good students can also use feedback to adapt their
learning to match the instructional methods being used. Both kinds of adaptation
require feedback from quizzes, assessments, measurements, and observations. To
do this well, the feedback should be compared with the standards for what’s intend-
ed to be learned and instruction adjusted, including instructional feedback, to the
individual learner.


Multiple Feedback Loops
Remember that a system is an assembly of multiple subsystems. Those systems work
independently but also contribute something to the overall system. Additionally, it’s
important to think of a system as being very dynamic. Each subsystem may have its
own feedback control loops as well as the system itself. Similarly, effective learning
has multiple, nested, feedback loops. An inner loop is feedback that a learner receives
immediately while doing a learning activity. An outer loop is feedback that a learner
gets between learning activities, such as new assignments or suggestions for what to
do next. Additional loops can guide the learner on the best paths toward long-term
learning goals connected with personal and career goals. Figure 4.4 shows learner
feedback loops aligned to the levels defined by the Advanced Distributed Learning
(ADL) Initiative’s Total Learning Architecture 10 and as considered in the IEEE’s
Conceptual Model for Adaptive Instructional Systems.
      We can visualize the innermost feedback loops in a process model as shown in
Figure 4.5. In the inner loop, the learner receives feedback while doing an activity.
The outer loop uses data from the activity and other data to inform macro-level
feedback—guidance on the selection of the next activity.
      Now let’s consider three more insights we can gain from control theory: the
138 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



                                             ER TRAJECT
                                      E CARE                OR
                                  TUR                          IES
                                FU      EN T C AR E ER T R AJE
                                    URR                       CT
                                   C         NT IAL  LE          OR
                                        EDE             VEL        Y
                                      CR
                                               N  LE VEL
                                          SSO
                                        LE
                                              TIVITY LE
                                           AC            V




                                                                  EL
                                                                                      LIFE-LONG
                                                                                LEARNING LOOPS
