                                                              LEARNE



     FIGURE 4.4. Learner-centered adaptive instructional systems
     may provide multiple learner feedback loops




                     OUTER
                      LOOP
                                                             PERFORMANCE
                                                                 DATA



                                         A LEARNING ACTIVITY


           ACTIVITY                                 EXERCISE                 FEEDBACK
          SELECTION



                                                                                      INNER
     FIGURE 4.5. Inner and Outer Loops                                                 LOOP
     Adapted from “The Behavior of Tutoring Systems” by Kurt VanLehn, 2006
                                           Learning Engineering is Engineering | 139



role of a transfer function, speed and frequency of feedback, and sensitivity to what
is being measured.


Transfer Functions
A transfer function is a mathematical description of the relationship between the
input and the output of a system. Let’s use the automobile example. The input is the
position of the gas pedal (more correctly called the accelerator pedal, as we’ll see in
a moment). The output is the speed of the car. Pressing the pedal to a certain position
doesn’t directly make the car go a corresponding speed. Rather, pressing the pedal
causes the car to accelerate at a rate proportional to the pedal position. If you keep
the pedal pressed to the floor, the car will continue to accelerate to higher speeds
until it reaches the limits of its construction. (For calculus fans, this means that the
transfer function of a car’s drivetrain involves an integral.)
       The transfer function is important because it’s incorporated into the design of
the controller. When an engineer designs a controller, they use the transfer function
to anticipate what will be the result of a particular input. Typically, they take the
inverse of the function to determine what input is required to achieve the desired
output. Getting back to our example of a car cruise control, some versions have an
option to maintain a consistent distance behind the car in front, adjusting the speed
to match that car. The operator can choose which transfer function is used, consistent
speed, or consistent distance. For those drivers that prefer saving fuel over driving
at a consistent speed, there could be a transfer function based on fuel consumption.
       The educational and training equivalent of a transfer function is a learning
theory—a description of how a learning activity will result in new knowledge.
Learning theories help us select activities that will effectively help a student learn a
particular skill.
       A personalized learning system will inevitably express one or more
learning theories in the selection of activities. It’s important to align the theory
to the population of learners (or learner), domain, and context. There are personal
differences in the way each person learns. In engineering terms, this means that
each learner has their own personal transfer function. And that transfer function
may change over time. (See Chapters 2, 3, 9, 12, and 15 on the learning sciences,
human-centered engineering design, tools for human-centered design, and learner
motivation for useful learning theories for your learning engineering toolbox.)
We can think of people in a school as being parts of a system; feedback can go to
different parts of the system (for example, instructors, learners, tutors, or tutoring
140 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     software). The different transfer functions direct the feedback to different parts of
     the system. An adaptive system might even decide which transfer function to use or
     where to direct the feedback, such as to different controllers, to get optimal results.
     In learning, an adaptive system would direct different feedback to online instruction,
     to an instructor, an advisor or counselor, and to the learner.


     Speed of Feedback
     For feedback to be most effective, it must be frequent, fast, and rich. Feedback must
     come often so that course corrections are made frequently. It must be fast, ideally
     immediate; otherwise it’s too late to affect the learning process. And it should be
     rich. “Incorrect” is not nearly so meaningful as, “You misplaced the negative sign
     in step 3.”
            Frequent, fast, and rich feedback depends on frequent, fast, and rich assess-
     ments. In conventional schooling, assessment is expensive. It costs a lot of teacher
     time to compose, administer, and score assessments. This is an excellent opportu-
     nity to apply technology. Computer supports can minimize the effort required to
     compose, administer, and score assessments. And computers can tabulate the results
     into effective teacher and student dashboards. The result is a more personalized and
     effective learning experience. Or, as an engineer might say, more predictable output
     with a smaller margin of error.
            The time from the moment an output is measured to a resulting change in the
     input is called a propagation delay. In educational terms, this is the time from when
     a learner’s capability (such as knowledge or skill) is assessed until the moment their
     activity is affected by the feedback from that assessment. In a traditional math class
     the student does homework one day, submits it the next day, and receives graded
     homework back the next day after that. Thus, the propagation delay is two days
     (or two class periods). Fast feedback means a shorter propagation delay. With more
     immediate feedback, learners have the chance to refine their understanding and ca-
     pabilities more quickly. Measurements that require human grading will naturally be
     slower. Many online learning systems offer near instantaneous feedback.


     Frequency of Feedback
     Frequency of feedback is a measure of how often the output (such as knowledge or
     skill, in our case) is measured and feedback generated. In a traditional classroom,
     mathematics feedback is daily (or once per class period). Some traditionally taught
                                           Learning Engineering is Engineering | 141



courses may only have two or three graded activities in the entire course. However,
this may be a pessimistic way to measure frequency. For example, if students can
check their answers in the back of the book, then feedback is both faster and more
frequent. A more ideal condition gives feedback after each attempted answer, but
this is only better if it’s the right kind of feedback. Research suggests that timeliness
and quality of feedback both matter.11 Of course, this guideline must be applied
judiciously; it’s often worth giving a learner time to make it through to the end of
something instead of interrupting.


Richness of Feedback
A third component of educational feedback is richness. In mathematics, this might
be the difference between being told that an answer is wrong versus being informed
about exactly what mistake was made. In a writing course, it might be the differ-
ence between a simple score versus detailed feedback about how the student might
improve their essay. For job performance, rich feedback would give an employee
insight into how to improve performance and how to recognize an error in the future
and distinguish between a job well done and subpar performance.
       Students can influence all three feedback factors. For example, college English
students who seek help at a writing lab will get faster, more frequent, and richer
feedback than students who don’t make use of the resource.
       Control theory tells us that faster and more frequent feedback compensates for
inaccurate measurements and poorer transfer functions. In education language, this
means that if we can make feedback faster and more frequent then we can compen-
sate for a less-than-perfect learning theory and suboptimal assessments. Of course,
it would be nice to have everything—fast, frequent, and rich feedback as well as
good quality assessments and a solid learning theory. But it’s useful to know that
some of these factors can compensate for others. Additionally, we also know from
the learning sciences that too much feedback to the learner can be unproductive for
learning. We know, for example that too much new information produces cognitive
load, and we know that in learning there’s a level of desirable difficulty.
       Physical and electronic systems are designed using filters and dampers to
prevent over-correcting. For example, if the feedback to the controller in a car’s
cruise control system were too precise it would continuously apply the brake and
then the gas, the brake and then the gas. In signal processing, a filter is a device
or process that removes some unwanted components or features from a signal. A
damper may be used to reduce the amplitude of mechanical vibrations in a physical
142 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     system, such as a soft pedal on a piano or the shock absorbers on a car.
             One-on-one tutors have a lot of raw data in various formats and fidelity levels
     while observing a learner working on a problem. They watch carefully as the student
     makes mistakes. Great tutors will not immediately correct a learner but give them a
     chance to discover their own errors and self-correct. The tutor is, in effect, acting as
     a filter or damper and a controller.


     Sensitivity to What’s Being Measured
     Feedback loops are a very effective tool—so effective that if the wrong thing is being
     measured or the wrong feedback is offered then the wrong skill will be optimized.
     A recent manifestation of this are complaints in United States K-12 education about
     “teaching to the test.” The concern is that because summative tests are used to eval-
     uate schools then the only skills that will be taught are those that are on the test. This
     concern is valid. Multiple studies have shown that a focus on conceptual understand-
     ing results in better test performance than test-focused instruction.12
            The problem with determining the right feedback is also manifest in the com-
     bination of skills that a particular problem might require. For example, a mathemat-
     ics story problem might require reading, visualization, and problem-solving skills in
     addition to the ability to solve the resulting mathematical equation. To offer effective
     feedback to a learner’s wrong answer, the system (whether human or automated)
     must be able to detect which of these skills wasn’t applied properly. One way to
     determine the root cause of the wrong answer is to examine the answers given for
     similar questions. If the root cause still can’t be deduced with enough certainty to
     provide good instructional feedback, some adaptive instructional systems may se-
     lect other questions to test the possible root causes. In many cases, the analysis
     requires interacting with the student to discover the steps they followed to answer
     the question.
            It’s tempting isolate skills and only assess one at a time. There are two reasons
     why this won't work. First, most learning goals involve applying multiple skills to-
     gether. Second, the demand for some skills simply can’t be eliminated. For example,
     nearly every assessment requires reading and following directions. Similarly, most
     real-life situations require the application of multiple skills learned to varying de-
     grees of competence.
                                          Learning Engineering is Engineering | 143



APPLYING FEEDBACK LOOPS
To summarize, engineering offers us the following insights about using feedback in
education and training:
   ► Select and use appropriate models, understanding the limitations of each.
      Choose your learning theory deliberately and measure its effectiveness.
      Iteratively adjust the model based on data.
   ► Use multiple levels of control loops with various feedback mechanisms.
      Adapt not only to what the student has and has not mastered but to the
      individual learning patterns and affinities of each student. Provide quizzes
      and other knowledge checks as well as tests.
   ► Fast and frequent feedback can compensate for lower quality in other
      areas of the system. This can hide problems. You may think you have a
      good learning theory when, in fact, fast feedback is making the difference;
      however, it’s also an opportunity to make deliberate trade-offs.
   ► Ensure the control loops use the proper outputs as feedback. Be sure you
      are measuring what you think you are measuring. And remember that every
      assessment measures multiple skills.
   ► Feedback loops based on data also inform the iterative learning engineering
      process. (See Chapters 5 and 6 on data instrumentation and analysis for a
      deeper discussion on this.)



                                       !%"
The first course on the edX platform was a course on circuit design. In place of
lectures, the Open edX platform had learning sequences, which interleaved short
videos, text, exercise, and interactives.
        Engineering relies on prototypes before fully developing solutions. Often this
starts with paper prototypes that sketch out wireframes of an idea and then to
rough models before implementing a minimum viable product (MVP) that can be
tested with a small set of users. The MITx platform was designed as such a prototype.
Piotr was surprised when the first prototype worked well. The course team received
strong positive feedback from a pilot group of on-campus students, and a month
later, strong positive feedback from online students. (Indeed, 98.6 percent of alumni
rated the course as good as or better than an in-person experience.) Obviously, there
144 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     was a sample bias and experimenter bias to the early results, but they were sufficient
     to raise $60 million funding for the edX project.
            In the prototype platform, courses were designed based on constructive
     learning—closely related to but not to be confused with constructivist learning
     theory, which is the idea that people actively construct or make their own
     knowledge.13 In contrast, constructive learning uses flexible technology that allows
     learners to connect new information to their prior knowledge in the modality that’s
     most effective for them. A presentation might walk students through a derivation,
     but ideally, students themselves would do the work through interwoven exercises.
            In practice, the constructive feedback approach was too time-intensive with
     regard to professional development for course instructors. Anant Agarwal, who cre-
     ated course videos, also ran one of the larger research groups at MIT’s Computer
     Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory and was director of the laboratory.
     He had around ten hours per week to record videos and the original approach was
     a change from his teaching style. These constraints called for a different approach.



     ENGINEERING ALWAYS INVOLVES CONSTRAINTS. Every problem
     to be solved has limits that must be considered when designing and
     developing the solution. These constraints may include limited resources,
     time, or technology to develop the solution. Other limits can be based
     on the learner populations to be served and contextual factors in the
     environments where the solution will be implemented. Engineering design
     also must consider engineering tolerance—the permissible limit or limits
     of variation of any component in the system. Each module or component
     of a system should only allow variations that don’t significantly a!ect the
     functioning of the overall system in a negative way, within the intended
     implementation conditions.14


            Based on the constraints, most of the learning sequences in the edX circuit
     design course came to be designed as simple understanding-check exercises inter-
     woven with lecture-style presentations, albeit with a screencast of a slideshow deck
     annotated with a digital stylus. This change led to active learning—still ahead of
     traditional university courses—but not as much of a constructive learning approach.
            For the homework assignments in the course, Piotr did a survey of past
     problem sets for the MIT version of the course. He found that the vast majority
                                          Learning Engineering is Engineering | 145



of problems could be adapted into either numeric responses, circuit schematics, or
equation responses. He built technology to support equations and numeric responses,
while Chris Terman built a circuit schematic editor and simulators. Note that he did
not implement multiple choice questions, since those did not allow for the model
of mastery learning designed for the course, where students could make as many
attempts as they needed to answer open-ended questions.
      Jerry Sussman put an incredible amount of time into creating the problems
themselves, which were complex, multi-concept analysis and design problems. Of
note, many problems had multiple solutions. For example, given a very basic prob-
lem like picking a pair of resistors for a resistive divider that attenuates a voltage
threefold, all that matters is the ratio of the resistors. As a result, many problems
could be graded by short Python scripts. (While these set a high bar for expertise to
the engineering of the course content, a five-line script doesn’t add significant time
to content creation for experienced programmers. This further highlights learning
engineering as a rigorous, skilled discipline and team sport.)
      The platform was designed to support iterative improvement of courses based
on data. Every action in the course was collected, archived, and preserved, including
context in an engineered (and since well-documented) format. The system supported
A/B testing of learning activities, randomly assigning alternative experiences, and
collecting data to determine which worked best. It had a pluggable architecture for
components (XModules, later renamed XBlocks) which allowed for creation of a
variety of learning experiences. As of this writing, the open-source community has
authored hundreds of XBlocks that plug into the Open edX platform.


                                       !&"
Design patterns are repeatable solutions to commonly occurring engineering
problems. A civil engineer is familiar with common bridge architectures and
which types work best in certain contexts. In software engineering, design patterns
establish best practices and promote shared understanding that improves the quality,
consistency, and maintainability of code. Software engineers use named design
patterns so that everyone on the team can work together and employ consistent
coding approaches based on those known patterns. Learning engineering teams can
also use design patterns; for example, the learning sciences concepts in Chapter 9
lend themselves to design pattern templates. Here’s an example design pattern for a
deliberate practice technique (with constructive feedback) applicable to tutoring or
coach contexts.
146 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     Example Design Pattern: Deliberate Practice

     Deliberate practice with feedback is a pattern of learning often employed with a
     human or software-based tutor or coach. It supports learning and development after
     the learner has gained initial conceptual understanding of the problem space. It can
     be paired with other learning modalities such as worked examples and as part of a
     cognitive apprenticeship process. This design pattern helps learners retain knowl-
     edge, apply knowledge, and build expertise.

     Applicable Contexts: Artificial, online, simulated, and real-world environments

     Inputs: Data about learner needs and prior learning

     Instrumentation: During the learning experience, data are observed and collected
     about the conditions that require a learner response as well as each learner response

     Design Considerations: How will observed behaviors and learner performance be
     used to adapt the problem and / or problem space (performance challenge) based on
     learner variability and progress? Which implied learning events or behaviors are
     most relevant, such as demonstration of mastery, motivational factors, or trying to
     game the system?

     Pattern:

       1. Ideally, begin with a baseline prediction about the learner’s competence* and
          prior learning; use this to assign a problem or performance task at an appro-
          priate level of difficulty. If this information is unavailable then fill the baseline
          model through other means, such as by collecting data via a pre-test.
       2. Use historical data, algorithms, or human (expert) insights to predict the prob-
          ability that the learner will demonstrate competence on the problem or task at
          any given time based their prior learning, performance, or forgetting curves.
       3. Use a pedagogical or andragogical model about learner development for the
          desired skill mastery, for example, sequencing from novice to expert in a



     * Intelligent tutoring systems may use knowledge inference analytics, such as knowledge
       tracing, to establish predictions about learner competence. (See Chapter 18 for a
       discussion.) A reusable competency definition and measurement criteria may be
       predefined for the skill or task. (See Chapter 8.)
                                          Learning Engineering is Engineering | 147



      domain, controlling the level of difficulty for a task or problem, addressing
      common misconceptions, or controlling cognitive load. Iteratively improve
      this model using data collected from learners who attempt the problems or
      tasks.
  4. Assign or present problems or performance tasks at a level of desirable diffi-
     culty and with adaptations (like hints) appropriate for the learner’s variability.
  5. The learner responds to a problem or part of performance task.
  6. Observe the learner’s responses and indicators of, for example, affective states
     (like boredom or confusion), engagement, or system abuse (gaming).
  7. Give unrequested or requested feedback as needed. Sometimes the feedback
     is informed by a learner’s mistake or misconception demonstrated in the pre-
     vious attempt. Sometimes feedback is requested, such as in the form of a hint.
     In all cases, feedback is informative, motivational (considering the learners
     emotional state and response), timely, elaborate, effective, and strategic.
  8. Learner reattempts the problem task until completed successfully or the an-
     swer / task is demonstrated by the tutor.
  9. Assign additional problems or performance tasks with adjustments based on
     new data from the prior attempt. (That is, repeat the process from step 4.)
  10. Continue this process until the learner has demonstrated mastery of the re-
      quired competencies.


                                        !'"
The need to apply engineering principles to the development of learning experiences
and learning conditions has never been greater. Part of meeting that need is the
emergence of the learning engineer as a distinct profession. Traditional models
of developing and delivering instruction are increasingly breaking down under
changing conditions in the world, such as shifts in the economy and employers’
need for new kinds of talent. Schools, institutions, and training organizations are
already under enormous pressure to adapt. A global pandemic and innovations in
information technology have changed how everyone works and learns.
       What will it mean to be a citizen and contributor in a future world that’s
changing at an accelerating rate? How can systems of learning be developed to
equip people for this constant change? Even academic systems that were considered
state of the art for previous generations failed to meet the needs of many. How can
148 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     the science of learning be applied at scale so that all people have opportunities to
     reach great learning outcomes, opportunities that thus far have been afforded only to
     some? Just like it took engineering to scale the production of penicillin during World
     War II, and it took engineering to produce and distribute the COVID-19 vaccines, it
     will take engineering to develop and scale the systems of learning that are needed by
     future populations of humanity.




           A professional engineer is competent by virtue of
           his / her fundamental education and training to apply
           the scientific method and outlook to the analysis and
           solution of engineering problems.

            . . . His / her education will have been such as to make
           him / her capable of closely and continuously following
           progress in his / her branch of engineering science
           by consulting newly published works on a worldwide
           basis, assimilating such information and applying it
           independently.

            . . . His / her education and training will have been such
           that he / she will have acquired a broad and general
           appreciation of the engineering sciences as well as
           thorough insight into the special features of his / her
           own branch.


                                 – Conference of Engineering Societies
                                    of Western Europe and the United
                                            States of America, 1961 15
• Science aims to discover truths about the world. Engineering aims
  to create scalable solutions to problems that work within a range of
  conditions. Engineering is a systematic problem-solving process.

• Constraints (like cost or time) and trade-o!s are central to engineering.

• Learning engineering requires engineering mindsets, including a systems
  perspective, ability to envision the future, and a scientific approach.

• Systems are designed using models of various degrees of fidelity.

• Scalability of complex systems is achieved in part by breaking them
  into modules, with interfaces between those modules. Interoperability is
  improved by using standard interfaces.

• Parts of a system have design constraints and tolerances.

• In an open-loop system, the controller uses what it knows about the
  conditions to set the input. In a learning context, instructional designers
  use what they know about learners (like age and demographics) and the
  learning content (like media type and depth of knowledge) as inputs.

• In a closed-loop system, the controller compares feedback from the
  output to adjust the input. Similarly, in learning engineering, the outputs
  (how well students are learning) must be compared to the desired output.

• Negative feedback means measuring the di!erence between the output
  and the reference (goal) and adapting accordingly.

• Faster, more frequent, and rich feedback is generally better, especially
  for dynamic systems, and can even compensate for less-than-ideal
  conditions in other parts of the system.

• Use filters and dampers to prevent over-correcting.

• Engineered systems (and human learning) work best with multiple
  feedback loops. Each subsystem may have their own feedback control
  loops as well as the system itself.

• The science of learning is not being applied at scale. Therefore, we see
  the need for a new profession to produce scaled solutions.


                                  LEARNING ENGINEERING IS ENGINEERING

                    KEY POINTS
150 | Learning Engineering Toolkit

CHAPTER 5


Learning Engineering
Uses Data (Part 1):
Instrumentation
by Erin!Czerwinski, Jim!Goodell, Steve!Ritter, Robert!Sottilare,
Khanh-Phuong!Thai, and Daniel!Jacobs



./+012134/1321//02134254+4607-/554+18460+-92-/49:+94+66;2/54
9:/4;/+0121345-2/1-/54<52134:<=+1>-/19/0/84/1321//02134
8/52314=/9:787;732/54+184data-informed decision-
making49745<667094;/+01/054+1849:/2048/?/;76=/19@



Data-informed decision-making is an essential and integral part of learning engi-
neering. Without it, you may be doing learning design, but you are not doing learn-
ing engineering. Data-informed decision-making has two parts, instrumentation and
analytics.

   ► Instrumentation is the part of the learning engineering process responsible
      for designing, developing, and implementing the data collection used within
      the learning solution to inform iterative improvements to the learning solution.
   ► Analytics is the part of learning engineering responsible for analysis and use
      of data within the learning solution to inform iterative improvements to the
      learning solution.

Instrumentation uses sensors to capture data along with software and hardware to
process and store data for analysis.

   ► Sensors are human-computer or environment-computer interfaces that cap-
      ture data.


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-9
154 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



        ► Data pipelines are the software and hardware infrastructure for capturing,
           processing, and storing data.

            The learning engineering process is different from one person or team de-
     signing a course and later having other researchers collect data as part of an efficacy
     study. Learning engineering addresses design of data instrumentation while design-
     ing the solution. The process assumes the data will be used to inform at least one
     iteration of the design.
            The learning engineering process includes understanding what data to collect,
     designing, and developing instrumentation, and using data to inform iterative design
     and development. As we discuss in Chapter 4, engineering involves trade-offs. It is
     not always practical or possible to collect data that might be valuable, and collecting
     more data is not necessarily better.
            In addition to informing iterative design and development, some systems also
     use instrumented data to inform feedback directly to learners, instructors, and other
     stakeholders, including:

           • Feedback to learners and instructors during a learning experience
           • Feedback between experiences to help determine what to do next
           • Feedback that guides the learner on pathways toward longer-term goals
            In some cases, new instrumentation hardware or software doesn’t need to be
     developed. For example, online learning platforms may have built-in software to
     capture learner responses (taps, clicks, or keystrokes) and log that data with context
     metadata to a file or database. The learning engineering team developing a new ac-
     tivity or lesson can use that built-in capability. You may be surprised to learn how
     much about a learner’s experience and cognitive state can be derived from taps,
     clicks, or keystrokes without any special hardware. For example, a learner’s level of
     boredom, confusion, frustration, and concentration may be detectable with just data
     from a keyboard and mouse or touch screen.
            However, a learning engineering team should not assume that current data
     collection is sufficient to answer new questions. “Data must be developed using a
     sound theoretical framing about what it is that we are trying to measure, rather than
     trying to align existing clickstream and telemetry data with constructs after-the-
     fact,” according to the Society for Learning Analytics Research (SoLAR).1
            Other times, the problem to be solved by the learning engineering team re-
     quires development of new sensors, new properties, or new data pipelines to cap-
     ture, store, tag, and process the data. Even if the instrumentation exists, the learning
                    Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 155



        HARDWARE           SOFTWARE SENSORS                DATA        ANALYTICAL ENGINES
         SENSORS        AND CONTEXTUAL METADATA         PIPELINES          AND TOOLS

                                           WHO
                                  WHAT ACTION
                                 WHAT ACTIVITY
                                 WHAT CONTEXT
                                  WHAT RESULT
                                                 DATA TRANSFORMATION




                                SOFTWARE
                                 CAPTURE




   RAW INPUT                                                              DATA ANALYTICS



FIGURE 5.1. Hardware and software sensors capture learner actions. Those data are
captured, processed, and stored through data pipelines for use within various analytical
engines and tools.
CC-BY Jim Goodell



engineering team must understand what subset of data is relevant to the current
challenge and what conclusions can be drawn from it.
       Chapter 1 explained that data are important in all parts of the learning engi-
neering process. Curating and defining the kinds of data to be instrumented is part
of understanding the challenge. Instrumentation design and development happen in
the creation phase. The instrumentation is put into use during the implementation
phase to collect data, and instrumented data are used within the investigation phase
to discover opportunities for improvement (new challenges).
       In the challenge and creation phases, a learning engineering team might
consider data requirements as modular challenges leading to the development of
instrumentation. Considerations include the following:

       1.   Question: What’s the question we want answered? Who cares? Why?
       2.   Method: How can we answer it using data? What analysis techniques?
       3.   Metrics: What do we need to calculate from the data to answer?
       4.   Data: What specifically defined data elements are needed for the
            metrics? What algorithm will be used?
156 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



            5. Sources: What systems already collect the needed elements or what new
               instrumentation is needed?
            6. Quality: Check assumptions about the existing sources and
               instrumentation, for example, with data profiling tools. Was it intended
               for a different purpose? Does the quality hold up for the new purpose
               and metrics? Are there other aspects of quality that will impact use of
               existing sources and instrumentation for this new purpose?

            In this chapter, we will examine instrumentation stories from the US military,
     an innovative university, and a company that makes software for children ages three
     to ten. We also will examine open source and standards-based platforms for instru-
     mentation of online, game-based, and experiential learning data.


                                                                                                        What data will
                                                                                                         answer our
                                                                                                      questions? What
                                                                                                     instrumentation do
                                                                                                     we need to collect
                                                                                TE
                                                                                   AM                        it?
                                          S
                                       ER
                                                                                    CREATION
                                   N




                                 What questions do
                                AR




                                 we want the data                                including designing,
                             LE




                                    to answer?                                 building solutions, data
                                                                                   instrumentation,
                                                                                implementation plans



                                                       CHALLENGE
                                                        Understand the
                                                     opportunity to improve
                  INVESTIGATION                       learning or learning
                    with data analysis                     conditions




        What do the data
       tell us? What other
       data could support
              iterative                                                   IMPLEMENTATION
          improvement?                                                        with data collection




                                                         CONTE X T
                                                                                                 Are we getting the
                                                                                                  data we need?

     FIGURE 5.2. Data are considered within all parts of
     the learning engineering process.
     CC-BY Aaron Kessler
                  Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 157




                                         !!"
Flight simulators at Boeing, language learning apps like Duolingo, learner
positioning systems like Gooru, and software for middle school mathematics like
ASSISTments and MATHia are all adaptive instructional systems. These systems
capture data about learner experiences and responses and use those data to adapt the
learning experiences. They collect the data using sensors.
       In the case of military training, sensors can be things like heart rate monitors.
For example, well-trained snipers must learn to control their heart rate and shoot in
between heart beats.2 An adaptive instructional system for a sniper might monitor
their heart rate, physical orientation of the weapon, and the accuracy of their shot.
       Training for soldiers, athletes, and astronauts uses many other kinds of
physiological sensors, such as galvanic skin response sensors to measure sweat (an
indication of stress), eye tracking (to monitor what the trainee is paying attention
to), and respiratory sensors. Just because your task must be measured doesn’t mean
your sensor is up to the job. Sensors have limits. One such limit is that sensors can
be obtrusive. Obtrusive sensors might be attached to the body of the learner or very
close to it. On the other hand, unobtrusive sensing means that learners usually are
not aware of the sensor or its function during use. Over time, body-worn sensors that
might be obtrusive at first can become unobtrusive if they aren’t causing discomfort.
       When designing sensor suites to collect data about individual learners or teams,
context matters. It is generally OK to be a bit obtrusive when you are exploring the
efficacy of sensor data as a basis for some inference or prediction of learner states
(for example, emotions, stress or other learner states that influence learning) as long
as it doesn’t interfere with the performance of the task being learned. If you need to
implement your solution in an operational environment, make sure that the sensor
hardware doesn’t interfere with their job.
       Bob Sottilare is familiar with the use of sensors. His work supports the largest
training organization in the world: the US military. At the US Army Research Lab-
oratory, he developed automated authoring, instructional management, and analysis
tools and intelligent tutoring systems (ITSs) and standards for adaptive instruction-
al systems. He is the father of the Generalized Intelligent Framework for Tutoring
(GIFT), an award-winning open source, artificial intelligence–based adaptive in-
structional architecture (see www.gifttutoring.org).
       One of Bob Sottilare’s undergraduate students, while interning for Sottilare,
wanted to investigate the efficacy of measuring heart rate unobtrusively. A few
158 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



                                                  years before that, Bob had been at an
                                                  affective computing conference at the
                                                  University of Memphis and he ran into
                                                  Rosalind Picard, who ran the MIT af-
                                                  fective computing laboratory. (Affective
                                                  here means relating to moods, feelings,
                                                  and attitudes.) One of her grad students
                                                  had developed a method to use webcams
                                                  to measure heart rate. The webcams col-
                                                  lected data through red-green-blue chan-
                                                  nels. It turns out that if the green and blue
                                                  channels were eliminated and the camera
                                                  was pointed just above a person’s eyes,
                                                  the intensity of the red channel could be
                                                  sensed, and a person’s heartbeat could
     FIGURE 5.3. A West Point cadet               be visualized. At first, this seemed like a
     testing two low-cost EEG sensors             much less obtrusive alternative for mea-
     that measure electrical activity of the
                                                  suring heart rate. However, sensors have
     brain and a low-cost eye tracker
                                                  limits. In this case, distance was a major
     Credit: Robert Sottilare
                                                  limitation. As the user moved away from
                                                  the camera, the number of pixels focused
     on the sweet spot of heavy blood flow between and above the eyes lessened and so
     did the accuracy of the heart rate monitor.
             Bob’s intern wanted to see if he could duplicate the informal results explored
     by Rosalind Picard. Bob and his student set up an experiment in the lab to see if they
     could accurately measure heart rate with this method. Like the original experiment,
     they found that distance had a huge effect on accuracy, but there were other factors
     that they didn’t think about beforehand. Low ambient light also affected accuracy.
     They noticed that the experimental trials early in the day showed more accuracy.
     They had the lights turned down in the lab, but the lab faced east, and the morning
     sun meant more ambient light in the morning and less later in the day. Even when a
     sensor is designed to measure a specific factor, such as heart rate, other conditions
     can influence the results. This was especially true in this case. These sensors not
     only suffered in accuracy due to the environment, but also failed to be unobtrusive
     (which was their intended use) because the camera needed to be close to the person’s
     forehead.
             Another time, Bob was working with others to conduct another experiment.
                  Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 159



They were collecting galvanic skin response data using Bluetooth. They had run
several trials, which were ruined by dropout (no data available) because the Blue-
tooth communication failed. They also ended up with intermittent and low-quality
data. Sensors have limits. One such limit is that some sensors are not suited to a task,
even if it may seem so at first. Testing sensors to make sure they give desired data
before implementing those sensors is important. Additionally, one sensor may not be
able to give the desired data. Sometimes it takes a mix of sensors to capture all the
data you need to predict a set of states.
       Even if the sensor is suitable for the task, the technology to collect the data
from the sensor may be a limiting factor. For example, an enormous amount of
interesting data could be captured from simultaneous video streams from millions
of users of an app, but the data pipeline required to capture all that data might be
prohibitive. Most solutions require less data to answer the questions related to the
learning engineering challenge. Trade-offs are required.


Sensors and their data pipelines have limits and require trade-offs.


       Another limiting factor is that sensor data can be perishable, just like that donut
you ate this morning. Fresh donuts and fresh data as you need them are always best.
For example, for some military training it may be important to know the person’s
level of anxiety and how well they are coping with a stressful situation. If you have
a really accurate model of the learner’s anxiety based on physiological sensor data,
then it is only good while the individual learner’s physiology is not influenced by
some external factor, like caffeine intake. Since people eat or drink all kinds of
things that change their heart rate, blood pressure, brain function, and other factors.
It is highly likely that trying to use yesterday’s data today will result in significant
errors. In the case of a sniper, starting to drink coffee could completely change what
their biological data look like.


DATA ARE PERISHABLE. A model of the learner’s current state based on
sensor data becomes less accurate with every new experience or external influence.


      The US Army also has developed sensors that capture other kinds of data
such as the radio communication between members of a squad. A team works best
when communication is efficient. These sensors can encode the communication
160 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     to determine how well the team is following communications protocols. Those
     data support constructive feedback during after action reviews to help the squad
     improve its performance. “The After Action Review (AAR) was developed by the
     United States Army in the 1970s to help its soldiers learn from both their mistakes
     and achievements.” 3 The encoding might include what command was given, what
     response was given, the time between command and response, and the contextual
     conditions that prompted the exchange.
           This encoding of raw sensor data resembles the encoding of raw log data col-
     lected by learning apps like Duolingo. The raw data capture that you tapped on the
     screen, and the encoded data add meaning, such as which word you tapped, what
     prompt you were given, when the question was displayed, how long it took you to
     respond, and other information about the prompt that helps Duolingo predict if you
     will get the answer right the next time you are asked to translate the same word.


                                           !""
     Data standards help streamline the development of instrumentation and ensure
     that data collected are comparable. One suite of standards now being maintained
     by the IEEE Standards Association is called Experience Application Programming
     Interface (xAPI) as described in Figure 5.4. Others like IMS Global’s Caliper™ and
     frameworks that have served as de facto standards like the ADAGE Framework are
     described later in this chapter.
           You may be thinking, “I’m not an experienced software engineer. Isn’t instru-
     mentation using standards like xAPI only for full-time geeks?” Not according to
     Anthony Altieri, founder of Omnes Solutions. In a 2017 Learning Solutions article,
     he showed how instructional designers can send an xAPI statement from a web page
     to a Learning Record Store (LRS) with just four steps and just four lines of Java-
     Script code.4

           1. Define a variable that holds the URL address of the LRS and the
              username and password to authenticate
           2. Tell the browser to use that variable for the LRS
           3. Define the xAPI statement
           4. Send the statement

           It may be easier if all you are doing is editing some values in a template web
     page. Chapter 13, Data Instrumentation Tools, includes a simple example.
                       Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 161
EXPERIENCE API (XAPI) AND THE LEARNING RECORD STORE (LRS)
   xAPI is an open set of standards used in training and education applications
   for instrumentation. API stands for application programming interface—a
   way for two programs, systems, or apps to communicate.




                                                                           API
                                                                                                   LRS
                 Learner - Paused - Video - at position 23:12




   xAPI is used in both distributed learning and traditional contexts, such as
   courses delivered via learning management systems. First developed by the
   US military’s Advanced Distributed Learning Initiative, xAPI is now part of a
   suite of international standards maintained by the IEEE Standards
   Association.
   xAPI data are structured as statements with these parts: an actor (for
   example, learner), a verb (for example, tapped/clicked), an object (for
   example, a button), with a timestamp, and other relevant info. Consider the
   following examples:

   ACTOR          VERB         OBJECT            RESULT         CONTEXT            TIMESTAMP
   “a learner”    “paused”     “this video”                     “position 23:56”   “2022-06-09T11:20…”

   “a learner”    “answered”   “this question”   “3”            Question URL       “2022-06-09T11:22…”

   “system”       “scored”     “this question”   “correct”      Question URL       “2022-06-09T11:22…”



   The standard support Profiles for different kinds of learning interactions, for
   example, watching a video, answering a test question, or reading an e-book.
   xAPI includes independent LRSs. Data from multiple platforms or activities
   may be collected into a common LRS rather than being locked in the data
   store of a learning management system.


  FIGURE 5.4. xAPI high-level summary. Open source resources are available through
  IEEE SA Open. xAPI Open Source Repository: https://gitlab.com/ieee-sa/xapi
  See also https://standards.ieee.org/initiatives/opensource/index.html
162 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                                            !#"
     In 2015, a data scientist was working in his office in Glendale, California. It was
     a large, open office crowded with desks, computer screens, and plush toys. There
     were whiteboards with technical sketches of databases sitting next to bookcases
     filled with titles like BIG 3rd Grade Math. Educators, game designers, engineers and
     researchers were hard at work building an educational game for children.
            Everyone was at their desk, focused on meeting the next deadline. It was quiet.
     So, the sudden shrill sound coming from the data scientist’s desk brought a lot of
     attention. Was it an air conditioner? A fire alarm? An airplane taking off? All eyes
     and ears turned to the data scientist’s desk.
            “My apologies, it’s my computer’s fan. The data are just too big.”
            That may have been the moment when big data had arrived at Age of Learning,
     and on the new project they were working on, which would come to be called My
     Math Academy.
            It was around 2015. Big data (and loud fans) had earlier arrived at mobile
     gaming companies like Zynga or Supercell. These mobile app developers already
     had developed a strong skill set in tracking how users were using apps, using data to
     segment users into different groups, and to predict their behavior.
            Video games were changing as well. Games weren’t just Mario and Zelda any-
     more. The things that people had learned about the motivational aspects of games
     were being applied in educational products since the 1980s, but the educational
     games industry had its ups and downs. But gamification was seeing a comeback
     through mobile apps. Gaming was once again coming to education in a big way.
            And educational technology was changing. Coursera, and several other pro-
     viders had launched recently, doing massively online courses. ABCMouse had be-
     come a hit success for educating young children. Education was moving beyond the
     textbook, beyond the print-sheet, and beyond the static website. Educational technol-
     ogy was embracing video and embracing interactivity.
            Age of Learning (the company that makes ABCMouse), was looking to do
     something new, and to use big data and video game design in an entirely new way.
     The three ingredients of education, video games, and big data were thrown into an
     oven and baked for several years, and a new product, called My Math Academy,
     would emerge.
                  Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 163



Lessons in Data Instrumentation
My Math Academy is a program designed to teach, practice, and assess basic math
skills for children between ages three and ten. It is composed of many small games,
each designed to teach, practice, or assess a particular skill. As they were building it,
the Age of Learning team knew that they wanted to do careful data instrumentation.
It was a key part of bringing big data, game design, and education together.
        This was because they knew about the potential value of having a large
data set. Researchers like Zoran Popović 5 and Ryan Baker had been successful in
capturing meaningful insights about children’s learning by analyzing data about
how they played video games and interacted with adaptive learning systems. (Read
more about Ryan Baker in Chapter 6.)
        There wasn’t really a big data set for children of this age with educational
data. There could be new insights about how people learn—for example, how often
do children confuse the number 3 with the similar looking number 8? Or how easy
is it to count if you can’t recognize numbers yet? They hoped they could apply the
insights they would gain to building a product where kids could not only enjoy their
experience but could also learn effectively and efficiently.
        Their first goal was to build a good, comprehensive data set for analysis. They
learned a lot of lessons along the way. They didn’t know exactly what they would be
looking for, so they opted to log with a lot of detail.
        Comprehensive logs were a challenge and a source of great debate. Some team
members wanted to log only what they thought they would need and would have a
clear use for. Others wanted to log every single event that happened, without any
plan for how to interpret the data.
        They settled on a plan, based on the ADAGE framework—an open API for
data collection in educational games. (See the inset on the next page for more de-
tails.) With ADAGE they would log down to a click level, and they would carefully
track all meaningful events in the game. They made sure to label things carefully, so
a human could read through the logs. They also tracked context, so an analyst could
easily figure out what else was going on, without doing a heroic parsing effort. In the
end, they got a little more data than they needed.
        The game designers’ and data team’s dialogue went something like this:
  Game designers:      Today we are going to ship a new game. Please help us
                       instrument the data.
        Data team:     Of course!
164 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



       Game designers:      Should we tell you what we want to know in advance, so you
                            can prepare accordingly?
             Data team:     No no, we will simply track everything. There is no need to
                            worry about it now!


     ADAGE FRAMEWORK. The Assessment Data Aggregator for Gaming
     Environments (or ADAGE) framework is a set of data collection and analysis
     tools on a shared, open source platform that uses big data techniques to trans-
     form clickstream data from game and design systems into formative feedback for
     usability testing and evidence of learning.6 The framework was designed to test
     whether click-stream data could provide reliable evidence of learning. ADAGE
     was used as a framework for video games in education research to demonstrate
     evidence of player learning. One goal of the framework and related research was
     to attend to the ways in which game play can provide a powerful new form of
     assessment. ADAGE consists of two main layers: (1) the semantic template that
     determines which click-stream data events could be indicators of learning; (2)
     the learning telemetry that captures data for analysis.7 The framework was used
     with and in popular game platforms and open source code repositories still exist.
     (See https://github.com/wids-eria/adage.)


             The data team settled on a strategy to track all the clicks within the games.
     They tracked to know if a child was clicking an important button or not. They tracked
     timestamps. They also tracked context, so they knew exactly what educational game
     the child was playing when the button was clicked.
             Things started well enough. A few days after they shipped, the game design-
     ers asked how many questions each student was getting right or wrong. They had
     the data. The logs had been appropriate, and it wasn’t hard to load the data into R (a
     data analytics tool set) to report the results.
             However, things soon turned. The next question was, “How long does it take
     to answer a question?” The answer was not in the logged data (because the students
     didn’t need to click when a question started). They couldn’t do the analysis without
     first reshipping the gaming and adding instrumentation.
             The data team learned a lesson: trying to track everything would track too
     much and not enough. The more questions you know in advance, the easier it is to
     track the right stuff. So, the data and design team began working together to try to
                    Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 165



figure out in advance what questions they might need from the data. And sometimes,
no matter how hard they tried, they just wouldn’t know until after they shipped.


LESSON: Instrumentation can be an iterative process.

      The team knew that analyzing poorly annotated logs was difficult, so they
chose to make the logs easy to interpret. The game designers wanted to know how
many games were passed successfully. They were trying to get a sense of if the
games were too easy or too hard.
  Game designers:       Can you tell us which games are easy and which are hard?
      Data analyst:     Sure, the data are all ready to go.

And so, the data analyst loaded the data into his computer. And he found all the
instances where the students had SUCCEEDED. And he added those all up and did
a very nice report.
      Data analyst:     Here is your report! I have counted all the SUCCEEDS.
  Game designers:       But where are the FAILS? Surely students SUCCEED and
                        FAIL, right?
      Data analyst:     No, they SUCCEED and CANCEL.

      The game designers were a little confused. “That does not sound right,” they
said. The analyst went back to his data. There were two columns. One was called
OUTCOME, and the other called SCORE. OUTCOME had two values, SUCCEED
and CANCEL. SCORE had two values, PASS and FAIL (and sometimes it was
empty too).

      Data analyst:     They should have called it COMPLETE and CANCEL,
                        instead of SUCCEED and CANCEL, and then I never would
                        have made that mistake.


LESSON: Language really matters, and it’s worth taking time to come up with
good data labels.


      The team knew that inconsistent logs were hard to work with, so they chose
to have clear rules for how to log data, and to have software that made those rules
166 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     easy to check and enforce. This was one of the hardest parts. Because the team was
     designing the game in an iterative way, they weren’t sure about all possible future
     features. They had to be careful with the naming conventions. This is a subtle art.

       Game designers:      We have invented a new game. It is beautiful and immersive
                            and educational. We should add data logs to it so we can
                            answer important questions and iterate on the design.
             Data team:     No problem. We’ll study this game more carefully and figure
                            out the best way to instrument the logging.
       Game designers:      It does resemble a multiple-choice question on an exam. You
                            could log it in a similar way!

     And so, the game designers left and the analysts played the game.

           Data analyst:    Before we discuss the instrumentation, let us order a pizza.

     The pizza was ordered.

           Data analyst:    It should arrive in thirty minutes. Now onto the business
                            at hand: I’ve played the game. The audio says, “pick the
                            number 3,” and it shows the student the numbers, 3, 4 and
                            5. The student must tap on the number 3. What shall we call
                            the number 3?
        Second analyst:     It is the correct answer!
       Another analyst:     No, it is the target choice.
       Another analyst:     No, it is the correct choice.
      First data analyst:   No, it is the target answer.
        Second analyst:     Or the chosen answer.

     And then the pizza arrived.

        Second analyst:     It’s been an hour and we have not named this simple thing?
                            This is taking much too long!
     Veteran data analyst: No, it’s taking about the right amount of time. I remember
                           when SUCCEED was confused with PASS. It’s worth
                           spending the time up front to get the language right. Let’s
                           make a decision, tell the developers, and keep our new word
                           and our common understanding of it long into the future.
                 Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 167



       This is an example where an existing standard or profile could have saved a
lot of time and provided labels that would be valuable across platforms within and
beyond the specific learning engineering team needs, not just across learning activi-
ties. And when a standard profile doesn’t exist it may be helpful to develop one with
others and publish it openly.
       In 2021 the ADL Initiative launched an xAPI Profile Server supporting
the open authoring, management, and publishing of xAPI profiles.8 xAPI profiles
provide a template for assembling sets of xAPI statements. These profiles model
the vocabulary, patterns, sequences, and concepts of learning activities within a
domain. Each xAPI profile is designed for a specific interaction type (for exam-
ple, video) or subject matter (for example, diagnostic medicine). While any given
learning activity could involve thousands of different data points about a learner’s
performance, an xAPI profile constrains what data are collected and in what for-
mats. This helps learning engineering teams to know what to collect, and it supports
interoperability—so that different systems use similar data collection schemas for
related learning experiences.


LESSON: Take time when choosing data labels, definitions and vocabulary. Use
standards when available; if standards don’t exist, help create new standards or
reusable profiles.


      The team knew that bad data are difficult to analyze, so they chose to invest
heavily in manual and automated quality assurance. They knew there were a lot
of people and layers involved in data instrumentation—from in the game, to in
the server, to the database, to the analyst. They made sure each layer had its own
documentation. Some months passed.
  Game designers:     Can you tell us if people are learning more, now that they
                      have played the game for several months?
      Data analyst:   Surely we have the data for this! We can do it.
   Veteran analyst:   (Now, a bit more “veteran.”) I don’t quite remember what is
                      a SUCCEED and what is a PASS.
      Data analyst:   I don’t really remember either, it’s been many months since
                      we thought about this. Let’s read through the code I wrote
                      several months ago and try to figure it out.
168 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     They read through the code, and eventually figured it out.

        Veteran analyst:    This same problem has come up twice. Let’s take the time to
                            write a data dictionary. It will define all the columns in our
                            tables, with all these little things that we figured out.
           Data analyst:    That’s great, it will also be much easier to share the data as
                            new people join the project.

           So, they created a data dictionary, where they documented each column, and
     the data inside. They used it many times in the future. There were a few good refer-
     ences they looked at for how to create good data dictionaries, including ceds.ed.gov.
     They also had the help of good software references, like metacat and metabase.


     LESSON: Maintain a data dictionary for your data definitions, aligned to standard
     definitions when available.


             The team knew that products change over time, as do technologies. They
     accepted that the data flow from an educational game, like water, flows down a
     stream. You can put some rocks here and there, but you can’t really control where it
     goes. And once the flow starts, you can’t really go back and change it, you just have
     to adapt. Using the data to change the app, and to help students learn, is the best part.
     After the game had shipped, the educators and data scientists and game designers
     all gathered.
       Game designers:      We built an educational game for three- to five-year-olds.
                            We have seen them play it. They love it, and they seem to be
                            learning.
              Educators:    Excellent! By the way, three- to five-year-olds, you say? A
                            three-year-old is very different than a four-year-old. That’s
                            30% of a life!
       Game designers:      Good point! Maybe we can look at each game and see the
                            pass rate based on the user’s age. If it’s very high or very
                            low, then maybe something isn’t right.

            And so, the data scientists took a look. Initially, they thought there wasn’t
     much of a difference between a three-year-old, a four-year-old, and a five-year-old.
     It’s the kind of thing that any teacher of children that age would say was obvious, but
                 Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 169



you might not know that if you were a mere game designer or programmer. When
they looked at the data, it was clear that users of a certain age would stay with the
game longer than other users. Four-year-olds would stay the longest, but younger or
older kids would drop off.
      They took a look at each game within the system and noticed that each game
had a particular age where the pass rate was around 75%. But it would be higher or
lower as you got further from the target age.
      Data analyst:   There does seem to be a correlation between cancellations,
                      pass rates, and age. Each game seems to have an optimal
                      age.
  Game designers:     Aha! Why don’t we show students their initial games
                      based on their age? Maybe people will have a better first
                      experience and will stick around longer.
        Educators:    Good idea!

      And so, it was done, and they saw that students stuck around longer!


LESSON: Data from good instrumentation gives visibility into opportunities for
improvement.



                                       !$"
Whether you or your team is using simple instrumentation, like data logged when
a learner submits a web form, or something more sophisticated, it always involves
some kind of sensor which may include a hardware interface (like a touch screen)
and software instrumentation. These sensor data then travel on a data pipeline and
are stored somewhere.
       Standards such as IMS Global’s Caliper, described in Figure 5.5, provide an
interoperable pipeline for collecting data captured from an online activity, such as
an activity launched from a learning management system using the Learning Tools
Interoperability (LTI) standard. IMS members include commercial learning man-
agement platform vendors that also provide analysis and data visualization software
features.
       IMS Global is a US-based 501(c)6 businesses association that facilitates stan-
dards development led by member-based work groups. IMS Global has testing
IMS GLOBAL CALIPER
Commercial learning management systems may use IMS Global’s Caliper
standard for instrumentation. It’s similar to xAPI in purpose and messaging
format but has a governed vocabulary and is engineered with a focus on cap-
ture and transport of analytics data tightly coupled with existing IMS teaching
and learning standards. Unlike xAPI, Caliper doesn’t specify a separate LRS
as part of the standard. In place of an LRS, IMS provides the Comprehensive
Learner Record Standard, a collection of discrete achievement assertions
(academic, co-curricular, and skills-based achievements) that can be issued
to the learner. The Caliper standard is built to complement existing IMS Glob-
al open standards and is well suited for use by organizations that use those
other IMS standards such as Common Cartridge, LTI, OneRoster and QTI.

Caliper includes a Sensor API for transmitting basic learning events in a sim-
ple and standardized format. Also, like xAPI, Caliper includes defined profiles
that model learning or supporting activities. As of April 2021, the IMS Global
website listed the following standard profiles which it also certifies for techni-
cal compliance:

Annotation Profile            Grading Profile                Session Profile
Assessment Profile            Library Profile                Survey Profile
Assignable Profile            Media Profile                  Tool Launch Profile
Basic Profile                 Reading Profile                Tool Use Profile
Feedback Profile              Resource Mgmt Profile
Forum Profile                 Search Profile

At the IMS Quarterly meeting, on August 15, 2016, IMS members and xAPI
developers determined that Caliper and xAPI are not equivalent, they have
di!erent origins, and there may be cases where one or the other is preferred,
as well as cases that support both or a translation of data from both. While
both xAPI (Actor/Verb/Object) and Caliper (Actor/Action/Activity) use a data
model based upon a triple statement structure, there are di!erences in the
detailed structure and usage of the Object and Activity definitions. However,
it should be possible for each specification to make use of the other’s Verb/
Action. The most significant di!erences noted in analysis are in the transport
layers for the specifications.

FIGURE 5.5. Caliper is the IMS Global Learning Consortium’s set of
standards for learning metrics. See www.imsglobal.org/activity/caliper
and www.imsglobal.org/initial-xapicaliper-comparison
                 Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 171



programs that enable products to be certified compliant with specific standards and
features of the standards. IMS conformance certification for Caliper Analytics is
available for Contributing and Affiliate-level members.
      IMS Global offers its member organizations a certification service for testing
applications that emit Caliper messages over HTTP. Each Metric Profile defined in
the Caliper Analytics specification serves as a separate unit of certification. Edtech
suppliers may certify their products for one, some, or all relevant Metric Profiles.9
For example, vendors that offer assessment products might certify a product as hav-
ing a compliant sensor API for the Caliper Assessment Profile designed to track at-
tempts, completion times, and item skips and responses as learners take assessments
and engage with individual assessment items. A learning management system ven-
dor would certify its platform as being compliant to receive those assessment event
messages from compliant sensor API.
      Chapter 13 has process tools for deciding what data to collect and for design-
ing instrumentation for the collection of those data. The chapter also includes refer-
ences to open-source software resources for instrumenting learning experiences.
    • Instrumentation includes “sensors” to collect data at the source and a
      “pipeline” to move, process, and store those data.
         – Sensors are hardware and software human-computer or
           environment-computer interfaces, like a touch screen on a phone
           and the software that handles the taps.
         – Sensors have limits.
         – Sensors may already exist or need to be developed.
         – Sensors may be obtrusive.
         – Sensor data may be perishable.
         – Raw data from sensors can collect events like taps and clicks but
           other things can be derived from those observations, such as a
           learner’s level of boredom or frustration.

    • The learning engineering process starts with the challenge. Knowing in
      advance what questions you want to answer with the data should guide
      what data to collect and review.

    • Iteration is needed throughout data collection, review, and analysis.

    • Domain-specific frameworks help establish repeatable approaches and
      practices.

    • Data standards provide an opportunity for interoperability and faster
      development of data pipelines. Experience API (xAPI) with its Learning
      Record Store (LRS) and Profile Server provide an infrastructure for
      instrumentation supported by IEEE. Caliper from IMS Global provides a
      standard for instrumentation that’s tightly integrated with other standards
      such as LTI, which is popular for launching activities from within a
      learning management system.

    • Maintain a data dictionary and use standard data definitions.

    • Engineering involves trade-o!s.




LEARNING ENGINEERING USES DATA (PART 1): INSTRUMENTATION

KEY POINTS
                    Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 1): Instrumentation | 173
