CHAPTER 6


Learning Engineering Uses
Data (Part 2): Analytics
by Michelle!Barrett, Erin!Czerwinski, Jim!Goodell, Daniel!Jacobs,
Steve!Ritter, Robert!Sottilare, and Khanh-Phuong!Thai



./+012134/1321//02134254+4607-/554+18460+-92-/49:+94+66;2/54
9:/4;/+0121345-2/1-/54<52134:<=+1>-/19/0/84/1321//02134
8/52314=/9:787;732/54+184data-informed decision-
making49745<667094;/+01/054+1849:/2048/?/;76=/19@



This chapter is a high-level introduction to data analysis for decision-making within
the learning engineering process. It addresses the use of data for feedback to the
learner and people supporting learners, and it addresses the use of data to inform
iterative improvements to engineered solutions. It gives some high-level guidance to
non-statisticians as to how to think about data.

Learning engineers use data to measure the effect of conditions on learning within
a rapid cycle for improvement. This involves understanding the data from the in-
strumented learning (including the context around learning experiences) and how to
determine whether the quality of the measure is sufficient for the decision at hand.
Analytics—the systematic computational analysis of data—is central to the investi-
gation phase of the learning engineering process introduced in Chapter 1.
      Data-informed design decisions are needed because human intuitions about
what helps people learn are often wrong. Looking at data from simple trials can
keep the work focused on the most important design features and avoid costly di-
versions. For example, when Ken Koedinger’s team at Carnegie Mellon University
was developing a cognitive tutor (an intelligent tutoring system) for Geometry, they
wondered if they should build in a diagramming tool. In a lot of problems presented


DOI: 10.4324/9781003276579-10
176 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     to students, the system showed a diagram, and Koedinger was wondering, “maybe
     that’s an important part of figuring out how to solve a geometry problem—to be able
     to draw the diagram.” But before they invested in developing diagramming soft-
     ware, they did a study: “In some we gave the diagram, in some we didn’t, in some we
     didn’t give it but prompted them to draw it [on paper],” he said. “There was almost
     no difference at all, and we did that before we built the diagramming tool fortunately.
     And we decided not to build it because it wasn’t the root cause of difficulty.”


                                              !!"
     Would you think that you could do better on a test by studying less with cheaper
     instructional materials? That’s exactly what one learning engineering team found.
            At Kaplan Inc., Bror Saxberg was chief learning officer, overseeing the re-
     search and development of innovative learning strategies and consistent academic
     standards for Kaplan University courses and Kaplan products. Like Ken Koeding-
     er at Carnegie Mellon University, Bror and his team recognized that if there were
     questions that the current science didn’t answer, his team would need to do their
     own experiments. Although they looked to outside research to inform interventions
     and course design, they recognized that they lacked evidence about what worked
     for their specific student population and delivery model. So they created a research
     pipeline to test the impact of interventions on student learning and implement the
     best approaches at scale. “The Pipeline was conceived of in 2013 by Bror Saxberg
     and David Niemi, Vice President of Measurement and Evaluation for Kaplan, Inc.,
     along with colleagues who saw a need for Kaplan University to accumulate credi-
     ble evidence about what worked to measurably improve learning and outcomes for
     adult, online students.” 1 Some of the research had surprising results.
            Within Kaplan is the Kaplan Test Preparation group. This group is one of the
     leaders in preparing students for the Law School Admission Test (LSAT). Saxberg
     explains that about 25 percent of this test includes “challenging logical reasoning
     problems with complex question stems and distractors.” 2 The Kaplan Test Prepara-
     tion group knew that these problems weren’t only a significant portion of the test but
     also very difficult to master. Their idea, which seemed like an obvious one, was to
     use video to prepare students for these test items. The video they produced included
     embedded practice questions for students to answer and typically took more than an
     hour for students to complete. A workbook also was provided as a part of the video
     test preparation.
            It seemed a solution to the problem was found, but there was also an alternative.
                          Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 177



A Kaplan Test Preparation learning engineer considered that the logical reasoning
portion of the test was very complex and was likely to “overload the working mem-
ory of most learners.” 3 (See Chapter 2 for an explanation of cognitive load theory.)
This engineer believed that a video was not the best way for students to get a grasp
on these logic problems. Instead, he proposed students study worked examples,
which are examples of the logical reasoning problems found on the test worked out
and supplemented with expert commentary.4
       The worked examples were designed to limit split attention by aligning each
statement in the question to its explanation, allowing students to focus on one thing
at a time. It offered expert thinking for each part of the question and reduced the
explanation to the minimum required to understand the logic.
       At this point, two methods had been developed for helping students prepare
for the logical reasoning part of the test. To find out which method was more ef-
fective, Kaplan conducted a study. There were four different conditions students
were put in before taking a twelve-question post-test (see Figure 6.1). One group of
students prepared using the videos and workbook for an average preparation time of
about ninety minutes. Another group studied eight worked examples for an average
time of about nine minutes. Another group studied fifteen worked examples for an
average time of about fifteen minutes, and the control group didn’t prepare at all for
the post-test. An example of the worked examples is shown in Figure 6.2.


        EXPERIMENTAL CONDITION               AVERAGE TIME SPENT
        Test only (Control)                  0 minutes (no test preparation)
        Videos and workbook                  90 minutes
        15 worked examples                   15 minutes
        8 worked examples                    9 minutes

       FIGURE 6.1. Experimental conditions in the Kaplan LSAT study

       The study found that the two worked example conditions “worked statistically
significantly better at helping learners master” the logical reasoning problems.
Additionally, it was found that the video and workbook students didn’t do much
better than the students who didn’t prepare at all for the test. Perhaps most surprising
of all was that the group that did the best was the eight-question worked examples
group—the students who only studied for about nine minutes. That group did better
than even the group that took more time to study more worked examples.5 Figure 6.3
shows the study results.
178 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     WORKED EXAMPLE
      QUESTION                                              EXPERT THINKING
      Linguist: Most people can tell whether a              The underlying principle here is:
      sequence of words in their own dialect is             People can apply sophisticated
      grammatical. Yet few people who can do so             rules without being able to
      can specify the relevant grammatical rules.           specify those rules.
      Which one of the following best illustrates the
      principle underlying the linguist’s statements?
      (A) Some people can write cogent and                  Nothing here is about rules
      accurate narrative descriptions of events.            or applying rules.
      But these people are not necessarily
      also capable of composing emotionally
      moving and satisfying poems.
      (B) Engineers who apply the principles of             Making a distinction about people
      physics to design buildings and bridges               that can apply rules and di!erent
      must know a great deal more than do the               people who discover the rules.
      physicists who discover these principles.
      (C) Some people can tell whether any                  Correct. This is saying some
      given piece of music is a waltz. But the              people can apply the rules
      majority of these people cannot state                 (defining characteristics) for
      the defining characteristics of a waltz.              identifying a waltz without knowing
                                                            specifically what those rules are.
      (D) Those travelers who most enjoy                    The second part of this sounds
      their journeys are not always those                   a bit like the second part of the
      most capable of vividly describing                    linguist’s underlying principle
      the details of those journeys.                        (describing details) but it is not
                                                            describing rules. And the first part
                                                            is not about applying any rules, it
                                                            is just about enjoying something.
      (E) Quite a few people know the rules                 This one is almost the opposite of
      of chess, but only a small number of                  the linguist’s principle, people who
      them can play chess very well.                        know the rules but can’t apply them.

     FIGURE 6.2. An example for LSAT reasoning like the ones tested by the Kaplan
     team. The question is on the left, and expert reasoning is on the right.
     Adapted from Khan Academy, www.khanacademy.org/test-prep/lsat/lsat-lessons


            The takeaway from this example isn’t that worked examples are the solution to
     every learning problem or that studying less is better for learning. The takeaway is
     that the best solution in this case was one that took into consideration that students
     were working with complex problems that would overload working memory. The
     team developed a theory based on recent research about worked examples, designed
                             Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 179


                                       Average Study Time (minutes)         Average Post-test Score




                 Test Only                                   42.25


                                                                                                92.66
Existing Video Instruction
                                                          39.42


                                        15.36
    15 Worked Examples
                                                                  48.67


                                    9.00
     8 Worked Examples
                                                                      53


                              0            20           40             60           80                100


  FIGURE 6.3. Kaplan study results: fewer worked examples outperformed longer videos



  an alternative preparation method, tested it in context, and let the data decide the
  best option. The solution in this case likely can’t be applied to other cases; learning
  engineering research isn’t about discovering generalizable learning science findings.
  Learning engineering research deals with highly contextualized cases within spe-
  cific courses or products for specific learner populations. This research then can be
  applied to these specific cases to improve learning outcomes.


                                            !""
  Data Analysis is a Team Sport: Making Sense of Data
  The challenge for learning engineering is to improve learning or learning condi-
  tions. Data analysis and analytics help us to answer whether our creation effectively
  improved learning or learning conditions. The findings also typically pique curiosi-
  ty, generating additional questions for exploration.
         Edmentum is a company that provides research-based curriculum, assess-
  ments for learning, and educational services for K-12 educators and learners. The
  roots of the company trace back to the early 1960s and the University of Illinois
  at Urbana-Champaign under the name PLATO. The company created what many
180 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



                                      experts would agree was the first computer-assisted
                                      instructional system designed for widespread use.6
  PSYC HOME TRICS IS THE              Today, Edmentum serves millions of teachers and
                                      students and employs an active learning engineering
 SCIENCE OF MEASURING
                                      practice to evaluate the contributions of product
 MENTAL CAPA CITIE S AND              features, instructional curriculum and content, and
        PROC ESSE S                   implementation practices to learning outcomes.
                                      Edmentum’s learning engineering processes include
                                      roles across the organization, such as learning engi-
                                      neers, psychometricians, learning designers, content
                                      and assessment developers, implementation consul-
                                      tants, user interface and user experience (UI / UX)
    designers, data scientists, software engineers, and product managers.
           Roles in any particular learning engineering team or at any other organization
    may be defined differently even if the same titles are used. Edmentum’s learning
    designers are experts in pedagogy, instructional design, and the specific content
    being taught. Edmentum’s research data engineers manage the data pipeline and
    conduct ETL (extract, transform, load) processes that make raw data usable for
    research purposes. This role includes capturing feedback data and supporting ex-
    periments; they also have the technical expertise to help with instrumentation and
    sensors. Finally, Edmentum’s learning engineers bring expertise in experimental
    design and learning sciences, particularly within the context of online and adaptive
    instructional systems. They also have strong technical data skills. In some ways,
    their roles are akin to UX researchers—but with a focus on what learners are doing
    in a system to achieve learning outcomes (which Edmentum’s psychometricians
    help measure). These learning engineers test hypotheses with data analyses and plan
    follow-up experiments to test additional hypotheses where needed. When it comes
    time to examine data analyses and decide on next steps, each of these roles has an
    important contribution to make.
           Why so many different areas of expertise? While organizations may not use
    the same titles or organizational structure as Edmentum, interpreting analytic re-
    sults requires consideration of a large variety of facets. Let’s look at a simple exam-
    ple from Edmentum’s archives.


     SCENARIO
     Here’s the situation: The results from a formative assessment delivered immediately
                         Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 181



following online instruction are yielding a poor pass rate. In other words, students
who are asked to demonstrate knowledge on a topic—on which they have just re-
ceived instruction—aren’t able to do so according to the measure provided to them.
What’s more, the pass rate is no better than the scores on the pre-test. This is a
basic descriptive analysis, simply calculating the percent of students who pass the
formative assessment. Yet the interpretation of those results benefits from different
perspectives with far more effective resulting actions. For this simple example, a few
roles are critical to the discussion:

      Learning engineer: What are the potential causes of this result? Who
      should be consulted and what additional analyses should we pursue? What
      actions should we take as a result of this finding?

      Learning designer: Is there evidence that the instruction could lead to
      confusion or doesn’t address a common misconception? Is the skill being
      taught chunked properly? Are there elements of instruction that may be
      distracting or unintentionally increase cognitive load? Are there other
      pedagogical approaches for this instruction that may be more successful?

      Content and assessment developers: Is the instructional content and the
      assessment content aligned so that the construct taught is the construct
      measured? Are there errors in the instructional or assessment content? Are
      the pre-test and post-test measuring the same construct?

      Psychometrician: Is the passing score set properly to distinguish between
      students who have mastered the content versus students who haven’t? Is
      there evidence any of the assessment content has multiple correct responses,
      a highly attractive distractor that may indicate a mis-key, a scoring issue
      that isn’t correctly assigning credit to a correct response? Is there evidence
      of construct-irrelevant variance, for example, linguistic complexity
      incommensurate with a learner’s profile, accommodations not being assigned
      for students who require them? Is there evidence students are speeding
      through the assessment, answering at random, or are otherwise disengaged?
      Are the pre-test and the post-test at the same level of difficulty?

      Implementation consultant: Is there evidence teachers may be
      implementing the system in an unintended way, for example having the
      students take the assessment prior to instruction? Is this context, setting, or
      group of students unusual?
182 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



           Software engineer: Is there an error in our calculation of a pass? In our
           attribution of formative assessment items to the skill?

           UI / UX designer: Is there evidence of construct-irrelevant variance,
           for example, the submit button located in an unusual place? Do we need
           to include additional ways for students to engage in the instructional or
           assessment content, for example, universal design for learning.


            As you can see, a very simple analysis can result in alternative hypotheses
     and possible investigations. If the focus of interpretation is too narrow, it is highly
     possible that an impactful revision is missed. In the scenario above, Edmentum took
     multiple actions, including internal process revisions to better target and align in-
     structional and assessment content based on evidence-centered design principles, a
     UI update, and correction of mis-keyed assessment items. In addition, regular moni-
     toring processes were established to allow for ongoing and continuous improvement.
            As the analyses become more sophisticated, moving from descriptive to
     predictive and prescriptive analytics, it may become challenging for all participants
     to understand and interpret the analyses performed. However, the importance of
     bringing together a team for this interpretation isn’t diminished and may even be
     more critical as the methods themselves (for example, deep learning) can yield less
     immediate transparency. Therefore, those engaging in generating the analytics
     should invest in simple and clear communication of methods and results such that all
     relevant roles can join the party.


                                             !#"
     Learning Analytics
     For some kinds of learning, one-on-one tutoring works a lot better than group in-
     struction. In 1984, Benjamin Bloom and his students found that learners tutored
     using a certain approach performed so much better that a student likely to get a ‘D’
     based on classroom instruction might get an ‘A’ if tutored.
            Great human tutors and teachers are “mind readers,” able to infer in real time
     what a student is feeling (frustrated, board, engaged) based on close observation of
     posture and facial expressions. And by close observation, and through deep under-
     standing of how people learn the topic at hand, they can infer what the learner might
     be thinking when faced with a problem. They can infer misconceptions or missed
                         Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 183



steps in the process by closely observing actions, reactions, and answers given.
       The approach that Benjamin Bloom found was so successful with tutoring is
called mastery learning. With mastery learning, the student must master the cur-
rent learning objective before moving on to the next one. Bloom’s dilemma was that
the model of one-on-one human tutoring with mastery learning was too costly to
implement at scale. Learning analytics may help solve that problem.
       One thing that makes a difference in one-on-one tutoring versus group
instruction is the amount of data available to the human tutor. A tutor can more
closely observe one learner at a time. The classroom teacher must take in information
about many students simultaneously. The better that a tutor can “read the mind” of
students, the better feedback they can provide to guide the learner toward mastery.
       Ryan Baker, a professor at the University of Pennsylvania, studies how data
from online interactions can better be used to improve learning. Most of his research
is with intelligent tutoring systems, but any learning experience will do if properly
instrumented.
       Ryan is the founder of the field of education data mining and a superstar in the
field of learning analytics. His advice is to “have a data analyst [on your team] who
knows something about education.” He said the current data analysis tools make it
easy to make a lot of mistakes and not understanding the context also leads to wrong
conclusions.
       Baker and his colleagues have figured out ways of using the kinds of data
collected by tutoring systems like Duolingo and Khan Academy’s practice exercises
to gain real-time insights similar to those that human tutors have. This is possible
without actually watching the learner. It can be done in some cases just using data
from students’ interactions with a touch screen or keyboard.
       There’s value in human tutoring that can’t be replaced by a machine, but by
using data from instrumented learning experiences, tutoring systems combined
with human teachers / tutors are moving closer to the kinds of results that Benjamin
Bloom observed with one-on-one human tutors. Data instrumentation and analytics
can collect the data and provide feedback to learners directly or augment a teacher’s
ability to notice when a student needs a certain kind of feedback.
       Real-time analytics used from instrumented classrooms (for example, using
video) can be used to signal to an instructor when a student is bored or frustrated.
It augments the human capability of the instructor to process data on many students
in the classroom.
       Over a decade ago, Ryan (among others) discovered ways of using trans-
action logs to detect when students were gaming the system in online intelligent
184 | Learning Engineering Toolkit
    BIG DATA LEARNING ANALYTICS
    Big data are extremely large data sets that may be analyzed computationally to reveal
    patterns, trends, and associations, especially relating to human behavior and inter-
    actions.* Data from instrumented learning experiences have created unprecedented
    opportunities for analytics to find patterns, predict outcomes, and gain insights for
    optimizing learning. For example, a set of techniques called knowledge tracing is
    used to model each learner’s mastery of concepts or skills based on data collected
    from engagement with a sequence of learning activities. Knowledge tracing data can
    help provide learners with personalized feedback and can help learning engineers
    discover imperfections in learning experiences and opportunities for improvement.
           When looking for patterns in big data, the raw data may have millions of
    transactions from thousands of learners and many variables. This may be too big
    to process efficiently. Big data analytics uses big data reduction methods to find
    patterns or features in the data most relevant to the previously identified challenge.
    For example, dimensional reduction reduces the number of variables by eliminating
    irrelevant properties or combining attributes into fewer variables. For example, analysis
    that involves location data with both city and zip code could reduce the size of the
    data set by eliminating one or both if the analysis is not concerned with location. In
    other cases, variables highly correlated can be combined, data with missing values
    eliminated, or features are extracted from a source data set into a derived data set for
    further analysis.
           Machine learning is an application of artificial intelligence that provides systems
    the ability to automatically learn using algorithms and statistical models to analyze and
    draw inferences from patterns in data. In machine learning, data-driven predictions
    or decisions are based on a training data set used to develop the model, then a
    validation data set is used to tune the model and know when the model has had the
    right amount of training, finally a test data set is used to evaluate the final model.
           For big problems that cannot be easily solved by one team, artificial intelligence
    engineers issue a global challenge called a shared task. The development of Natural
    Language Processing—the technology used by Alexa, Siri, and Cortana—advanced
    much more rapidly than it would have otherwise because of shared tasks. It is often
    about getting the right data into the hands of the people who can, and are interested
    in, solving a problem.
           In learning engineering, the research team at Duolingo used a shared task to
    find out which learning algorithms could best predict what mistakes each learner will
    make in the future.† Anonymized lesson histories from millions of Duolingo learners
    made the research possible. In another shared task, participants started with English
    prompts and generated sets of plausible translations in five other languages. For
    evaluation, Duolingo provided sentences with handcrafted, field-tested sets of possible
    translations, weighted and ranked according to actual learner response frequency.
    These machine learning shared tasks helped advance the field overall while solving a
    specific challenge in Duolingo’s learning engineering process of iterative improvement.



  * “big data, n.1.” OED Online. Oxford University Press, December 2020. Web. Retrieved Jan. 2021.
  † https://venturebeat.com/2020/08/18/how-duolingo-uses-ai-in-every-part-of-its-app. Retrieved Jan. 2021.
                          Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 185



tutors, such as when they were doing practice
problems. Just like students might ask a human
                                                                D IG   INTO BIG DATA
tutor for help when they get stuck on a problem,
they could ask the tutor for a hint. Within each                   AN D LE AR NING
                                                                                      YAN’S
problem, the tutor has a set of available hints                ANALY TIC S AT R
                                                                                        DATA
that increasingly get closer to revealing the                  F RE E MOOC: BIG
answer. Sometimes rather than putting in the                         IN E DUC ATION
effort to practice, students gamed the system by
asking for hint after hint until the answer was
given. Ryan and his colleagues used analysis of
transaction log data to determine when asking
for a hint is productive for learning and when
it’s counterproductive. Further research has examined what kinds of feedback helps
bring about productive learner behaviors, and Ryan has also investigated interven-
tions to help nudge learners away from gaming and other unproductive behaviors
toward more productive learning behaviors.
        In 2006, Ryan learned a hard lesson, that quantitative analysis doesn’t tell the
whole story. The intervention was a puppy character, Scooter the Tutor, displayed
on the screen. “If kids use the software properly then Scooter would look happy, and
he danced around. If they misused the software Scooter, looked sadder and sadder.
Eventually if students had failed to learn and continued to game the system, Scooter
would give them supplementary exercises that were different in kind but covered the
same stuff they bypassed.”
        The idea was that it would reduce the incentive to game because there’d be
a direct penalty for gaming. “So, we deployed it, and lo and behold it worked! The
data showed that students gamed the system about half as much. And when students
didn’t game the system, they learned significantly more. Rather than falling further
behind, which is what we’d seen in previous studies, they actually caught up to the
students who hadn’t gamed.”
        This looked like a big victory. Ryan documented the positive findings from his
experiments in his doctoral dissertation and in journal articles.7 But the data didn’t
tell the full story. For a solution to scale, people need to use it. In this case, it turned
out, teachers and students didn’t like Scooter. And that was a surprise. “The school
system that we’d been working with didn’t want to use it because the teachers didn’t
like it. And so we dug a little deeper. We actually asked, ‘how did you like it,’ ‘how
did it affect your learning,’ ‘what did you think about it?’”
        By surveying students and teachers, the additional qualitative data revealed
186 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     unfortunate truths about the scalability of the solution.
           • “Students who never gamed, who only saw the happy dancing dog, said
             they liked it; they said it helped their learning, despite the fact that it
             didn’t.”
           • Students who gamed the system, who the data showed benefited by the
             intervention, said they didn’t like Scooter and that Scooter hurt their
             learning. “Now do I think they actually thought it hurt their learning?
             No. I think they didn’t like it, but they were willing to tell us (and they
             probably told their teacher) that they thought it hurt their learning.”

           This speaks to the importance of human-centered design (as discussed in
     Chapter 3). The other thing Ryan and his team learned was that Scooter promoted
     bad behavior. “Scooter got really angry and caught fire. It wasn’t meant to look like
     catching fire. It was meant to look like anger lines, but it looked like catching fire.
     And some of the middle school boys came up with this game that they played where
     they tried to see who could get Scooter to catch fire first.”




     You know how to use the tutor right!          Why do we need to know the
                                                   largest value of height
                                                   in this part of the problem?


                                                   To help pick a scale for the axis
                                                   To help pick the last label of the axis
                                                   To help pick the first label of the axis
                                                   To help pick which point to plot first
     Work carefully, so you can learn.

     FIGURE 6.4. Scooter the Tutor—looking happy when the student hasn’t been
     gaming harmfully (top-left), giving a supplementary exercise to a gaming
     student (right), and looking angry (unintentionally looking on fire) when the
     student is believed to have been gaming the system (bottom-left)
                         Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 187



       This is an example of data from an experiment showing that an intervention
can be effective in the context of a small-scale experiment, but it can fail to scale due
to contextual factors outside of the cognitive science. “It failed because we didn’t
consider the other dimensions of our solution and its adoption,” Ryan told us.
       There’s an interesting postscript to this story, however, that emphasizes the
importance of context. A key concept in the learning engineering process described
in Chapters 1 and 2 is that learners are contextually situated. Part of that context is
the culture of the school, organization, or community. After Ryan’s initial develop-
ment of Scooter the Tutor, researchers in other countries used translated versions of
Scooter to run studies themselves. “In some countries, they liked Scooter and found
him helpful. In some countries, the students never really got any interventions be-
cause everyone would go off and talk about the answer before they would even type
it into Scooter. It was really fascinating—the diversity of ways that students in dif-
ferent countries respond to the same agent. That’s another really interesting learning
engineering challenge; we can’t just assume that we build something once, in one
context, and forever that’s how it’s going to be.”

Data standards make it easier to create, share, and integrate data. They provide
data integrity, accuracy, and consistency, clarify ambiguous meanings, minimize
redundant data, and the means to document intended use and rules. To share, ex-
change, and understand data, we must standardize the format as well as the mean-
ing.8 See Chapter 14, Software and Technology Standards as Tools.




                                         !#"
As a cognitive scientist at Carnegie Learning, Martina Pavelko was used to thinking
deeply about the particular ways that students learn to solve math problems. The
compendium of strategies, misconceptions, skills and feedback used to guide stu-
dents through this process is part of the cognitive model that drives instruction in the
MATHia intelligent tutoring system.
       But now Martina was asked to look at this process from a different perspec-
tive. Carnegie Learning was going to try to simplify the process of creating these
cognitive models in order to make them more accessible to new cognitive scientists,
and to enable cognitive scientists focused on topics other than mathematics to create
new content. Martina needed to introspect into her own process—and those of other
188 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     cognitive scientists—to accomplish this task.
             Martina had also earned a masters’ degree in applied learning science, and
     she’d previously worked as a chemistry teacher. She brought the precision of a chem-
     ist to the task, and her academic preparation and years of teaching gave her the
     instinct to know what would work with students and be acceptable in the classroom.
             Authoring tools used to create instructional content make assumptions about
     roles and responsibilities in the authoring process, and one particular challenge was
     deciding where to draw the line between writing the cognitive model (the general
     AI system that can guide students through solving math problems) and writing the
     content (the specific math problems that students will need to solve). Consider a par-
     ticular word problem that a student could be asked to solve. Mathematical thinking
     and content are related; the story expressed in a word problem might lead to different
     misconceptions or strategic approaches than another, but writing a word problem re-
     quires a very different skill set than writing a cognitive model. A good word problem
     is written clearly and at an appropriate level for students, expresses a story that is
     interesting to students, and requires students to apply the appropriate mathematics.

     A cognitive model is a descriptive or computational representation of human
     thinking about a given concept, skill, or domain for the purposes of comprehension
     and prediction. A cognitive model of learning, is an account of how humans gain
     accurate and complete knowledge.9


            Martina quickly recognized that a key set of decisions for the new tool revolved
     around the relationship between cognitive model authoring and content authoring. In
     discussion with other cognitive scientists and engineers, she was asked what seemed
     to be a simple question: How many word problems do you need?
             Martina’s immediate answer was that standard practice was to write 200
     problems for each topic. No student would ever do all 200, but this database of
     problems seemed to be enough to cover the different skills that students would need
     to learn within the domain of mathematics as well as to provide enough variety in the
     problem set that students sitting next to each other would not get the same problem.
             Following standard practice was not a satisfying answer, and initial
     discussions with others focused on questions that reflected a mathematical approach
     to the problem: what’s the probability that two students sitting together will be
     working on the same problem? How many problems per skill do we need and how
     does this question change for skills that tend to co-occur? MATHia’s algorithm for
                         Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 189



picking problems for individual students is complex; it considers the student’s past
history, strengths, weaknesses and other factors, and characterizing the problem
mathematically seemed difficult. Since they were focused on authoring tools for
creating new content, it did not initially occur to them that existing data might help
them understand this problem. Instead of focusing on new content, they decided
to first understand better how their existing content was being used. Were there
mathematics topics that needed fewer than 200 problems? Were there ones that
needed more? More importantly, what factors influenced not only the number of
problems needed but the variety?
       Carnegie Learning hosts a biweekly data jam, a meeting where representatives
of different departments—including Cognitive Science, Research, Engineering,
Product Management and Marketing—get together to talk about difficult issues related
to understanding or collecting data. At Carnegie Learning, data analytics is a team
sport. Martina, along with Hao Li, a learning engineer in the research department,
took the problem to the data jam. The meeting led to a different perspective on the
problem. Instead of thinking about how many problems a topic needed, they started
talking about problem use. The right number of problems (and the right distribution
of problem types) should, ideally, lead to a situation where every problem is used
equally often. Problems that address difficult skills, where students might need to
complete ten problems to reach mastery need to be more frequent than problems that
address easier skills, where students might need only three or four to reach mastery.
By examining existing content, they discovered many cases where the problem bank
was less than optimal. In several cases, there were some problems that were rarely
used while others were used for almost every student.

Instead of focusing on new content, they decided to first understand better how
their existing content was being used.


       This project started with a focus on authoring tools for new content and did
not initially focus on analytics on existing data, but the emphasis on data changed
the project and the authoring process substantially. They moved toward a system
where, instead of trying to author the complete problem bank up front, they author a
minimal set of problems based on their assumptions about skill difficulty and other
factors. When the system is delivered, they monitor the distribution of problem
presentations to determine whether there are gaps in the problem bank that need to
be filled.
190 | Learning Engineering Toolkit




                                              !$"
     In Chapter 4 we examined techniques used across fields of engineering. One of those
     techniques is building models. In creating tutoring systems, data are used to model
     things about the learner cognitive states such as the ability to recall some knowl-
     edge or do some skill. Models are also used to predict a learner’s affective state or
     behaviors under given conditions. And models are used to predict how well a given
     learning activity will bring about a given learning outcome for a group of learners
     or an individual.
            A big part of learning analytics applied to intelligent tutoring systems has to do
     with creating, testing, and tuning predictive or descriptive models. Remember when
     Bror Saxberg and the team at Kaplan found that studying eight worked examples
     was more effective than studying fifteen worked examples? They also found that
     fifteen worked examples were better than, for example, zero worked examples.
            What if we want to predict the optimum number of worked examples? Figure
     6.5 shows the three data points from which we can build a simple predictive model.
     With just three data points and reported margins of error in the data from the original
     study we will not be able to get an accurate prediction, but we can use those three
     points to illustrate the concept of predictive modeling.


                    WORKED EXAMPLES                    POST-TEST SCORE
                               0                              42.25
                               8                                53
                              15                              48.67

                 FIGURE 6.5. Data points from the Kaplan study


            One way we can build a predictive model is to fit a curve onto the three points
     we have. The following analysis illustrates the concept of fitting known data points
     to a curve (a parabola). Remember the quadratic formula !" #" $% &" '" (%" '" ) ? We
     have three points that we can plug into three equations, and each equation has three
     unknown variables: a, b, and c. The quadratic function tool at mathepower.com can
     help us develop this model for the number of worked examples. And, interestingly,
     it also gives a worked example showing how to calculate the parabola formula from
                                Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 191



                                                  MAXIMUM
                                               TURNING POINTS
                                                                                  Here’s the curve fit to
You are looking for a quadratic             AT (9.122 , 53.1
                                                              51)                   the three points
function with points at:

THIS IS A SYSTEM OF 3 EQUATIONS WITH 3 VARIABLES,
SO USING THEM TOGETHER, WE CAN SOLVE FOR A, B, AND C

                                                                                  7&"&(2*2$    LINE-1
                                       b
                       To isolate the variable,              5& 3& 0*#2$6& 3& 0*0#+7&"&0*)2)   LINE-2
                  LINE-3 was divided by -13.125                     &6& 3& &/0*#427&"&#0*())   LINE-3

INSERTING THOSE VALUES SHOWS THAT THE FUNCTION EQUALS: & -!.&"&/0*#1#!2&3&2*14!&3&(2*2$
                                                                        f

FIGURE 6.6. Data points from Kaplan study plugged into the quadratic
function tool at mathepower.com
192 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     the three points! * Figure 6.6 shows the mathepower.com outcome.
            The solved function serves as a model predicting that the optimum number of
     worked examples is about nine (9.122 to be more precise). We can see an estimate of
     that by looking at the peak of the graph. We can also predict the scores of students
     that study more or less worked examples by plugging the number into the formula or
     looking at the graph. What might someone score if they studied five worked exam-
     ples? What about twenty?
            But this is just a model, and models are not perfect. Testing the model with
     additional real data from learners’ interactions is an important next step. With new
     insights from testing, we can iteratively refine the model. It would be expensive to
     test every possible option but data from testing even one or two additional conditions
     could give additional insights into the accuracy of the model.
            We can also add parameters to the model to make it more precise and then test
     again, such as the time spent studying each worked example. We can A/B test var-
     ious worked example features. With test data we can find outliers, such as learners
     who don’t do as well with nine worked examples. And from that, we can iteratively
     test alternative activity designs or other conditions that might work better for those
     learners.
            With machine learning, a kind of artificial intelligence, a predictive model
     may be trained using existing data that reflect the kind of variables. Chapter 18,
     Data Analysis Tools describes other kinds of predictive and descriptive models used
     in learning analytics and education data mining and techniques used to test those
     models.


                                               !%"
     Carnegie Mellon University has a large computer science and engineering cohort of
     students. Incoming freshmen have such variability in their math backgrounds that
     some struggle to hit the ground running in their first year as they attempt to catch up
     to their peers in the mathematics courses. This presented a challenge that learning
     engineering techniques could help address.
           The Eberly Center for Teaching Excellence and Educational Innovation is a


     * Note: In this specific case the data points had margins of error showing levels of
       uncertainty about the measurement. Fitting a curve to uncertain points leads to uncertain
       predictions, but to illustrate a simple predictive model based on fitting to a curve we’ll
       assume we have error-free data points.
                          Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 193



centralized campus office that helps faculty use learning science research, collect
data, and “ . . . design and implement meaningful educational experiences.”
In addition to the services offered by the Eberly Center, CMU has packaged its
educational technologies into a toolkit that supports the spectrum from learning
design through analysis and iterative improvement. This ecosystem of tools is called
the OpenSimon Toolkit.
       Using the OpenSimon Toolkit, Marsha Lovett, Director of the Eberly
Center, worked with her learning engineering, data science, and computer science
colleagues to solve the freshman math challenge. Specifically, they designed a set
of self-paced modules targeting the prerequisite skills that incoming students lacked
so that all students started with the same baseline competency. The system was also
instrumented to provide data to faculty about the areas where new students might
still be weak.
       Marsha and her team built three modules and packaged them into a course
called Discrete Math Primer. They used the Open Learning Initiative platform
within OpenSimon; it’s a kind of online textbook-replacement courseware. Each
of the Discrete Math Primer modules targeted a primary math skill: logic, sets, or
functions. Each module included practice and assessment questions that provided
data about students’ number of attempts, correctness, and need for hints. Pre- and
post-tests measured learning gains in a summative fashion.
       One way to model learning is using a learning curve that shows the rate
of a person’s (or group of people’s) progress at learning a new skill over time.
Learning curves show what we can’t necessarily see with just the raw data. Using



        100

             90
Quiz Score




             80
                                                              FIGURE 6.7.
                                                              2016 post-test data
             70                                               from CMU’s Discrete
                                                              Math Primer
             60

             50

                  Logic          Sets         Functions
194 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     LearnSphere 10 as a data repository and analysis infrastructure, and DataShop 11
     analysis tools for generating learning curves, CMU could chart the learning or lack
     thereof taking place. These analyses also provided key information about which
     skills students needed more practice with as well as where there were holes in the
     analytics being collected.
            The course was first offered to students in the summer of 2016. In the first
     delivery of the course, they found that students were generally improving, according
     to post-test data at the conclusion of the modules—except in functions. (See Fig-
     ure 6.7.) However, using learning curve analysis on each individual skill, the data
     appeared to be more nuanced than this. The learning curves in Figure 6.7 provide
     a visualization of the data for two of the skills: logic and functions. The number of
     opportunities to practice a skill is shown along the x-axis, and the error rate is on the
     y-axis. With each attempt at practicing the same skill, the number of errors a student
     will generate should decrease.
            Ideally, learning curves should generally look like the dotted line shown in the
     functions module graph on the right in Figure 6.8. It starts high and goes down in a
     smooth line. For the logic module learning curve (on the left of Figure 6.8), learning
     doesn’t seem to be occurring—students aren’t improving with this skill over time.
     And although the learning curve for the functions module looks better, it has some
     strange anomalies where the error rate goes back up, such as right before the fifth
     and fifteenth attempts. These anomalies show where learning isn’t taking place as
     expected, but they don’t tell us the causes of the anomalies. Finding the cause of the
     anomalies requires more investigation. It could be that learning can be improved
     by providing more practice opportunities for a given skill, more scaffolding in the
     course in the form of more or better instruction, or by breaking down the skills fur-
     ther to provide more practice opportunities in finer-grained skills. These are just a
     few of the next steps that were taken on this Discrete Math Primer.
            With a few new refinements for the course identified, Marsha and her team
     went to work. They added more practice and feedback on targeted skills and
     improved the summative assessments. The improved course was delivered, and
     more data were collected and analyzed in the summer of 2017. This analysis and
     subsequent improvement provided even better results.
            CMU departments were pleased. The changes to the course, targeting individ-
     ual skills for improvement interventions, led to a significant increase in the students’
     understanding of functions.
            But there was still room for iterative improvement. Data from subsequent
     courses showed that students were still struggling with transfer of the concepts to
                                          Learning Engineering Uses Data (Part 2): Analytics | 195



                                                  STUDENT ERROR RATE
                       E
LEARNING CURV                                      (SOLID)
     (DOTTED)
    Error Rate




                                                              Error Rate
                                   Time                                          Time

                   Logic Module                                            Functions Module
                     PROBLEMATIC                                                GOOD

   FIGURE 6.8. 2016 learning curve analyses from CMU’s Discrete Math Primer. The
   right side shows that learning is taking place (as error rates fall over time), while
   the left side indicates an issue with the learning or assessment experience.




                         100

                              90
                 Quiz Score




                              80

                              70

                              60

                              50

                                          Logic           Sets                Functions

                                    SUMMER 2016        SUMMER 2017             SUMMER 2018



   FIGURE 6.9. 2016–2018 post-test data from CMU’s Discrete Math Primer
196 | Learning Engineering Toolkit



     open-ended problems. Since open-ended questions are difficult to auto-grade in an
     online course, the next improvement step was to add cognitive tutor authoring tools
     to the course. These tools provides an interface for developing complex problems
     with multiple steps. By integrating them into the course, Marsha and her team were
     instrumenting it for finer-grained data collection during each step in a problem. For
     the students, cognitive tutors provide practice in individual skills, with feedback
     given with each step in a problem. The results of this iteratively improved course
     resulted in even more significant learning gains for the students, evidenced by the
     following year’s post-test data. See Figure 6.9 for a comparison of the 2016, 2017,
     and 2018 course outcomes.
           CMU’s Discrete Math Primer is still in use today and is used by more faculty
     each semester. The success of this iterative problem solving also has resulted in
     the math faculty requesting more modules for their majors. Learning engineering
     processes have given the students a way to come up to speed with their peers, and
     the faculty a tool for supporting struggling students.
• Analytics, the systematic computational analysis of data, is central to the
  investigation phase of the learning engineering process.

• Data analysis is needed because human intuition is often wrong.

• Findings from the learning sciences can inform theories that can be
  tested by analyzing data from experiments.

• Research within learning engineering focuses on identifying highly
  contextualized findings about learning within specific products or
  conditions for specific learner populations.

• Learning engineering—and data analysis for learning engineering—is
  a team sport often requiring psychometricians, learning designers,
  content and assessment developers, implementation consultants, UI / UX
  designers, data scientists, software engineers, and product managers.

• Big data are extremely large data sets that may be analyzed
  computationally to reveal patterns, trends, and associations, especially
  relating to human behavior and interactions.

• Quantitative data doesn’t always tell the whole story.

• Learning is contextually situated and so are the results of any
  experimental data.

• Data can be used to question existing practices and assumptions.

• In learning engineering, data are sometimes used to model things
  about the learner, to predict a learner’s state or behavior under given
  conditions, and to predict how well a given learning activity will bring
  about a given learning outcome.

• Learning curve analysis shows a learner’s progress at learning a new skill
  over time. One form of learning curve analysis measures error rates on
  assessment problems; as a person masters a skill their errors should go
  down.




             LEARNING ENGINEERING USES DATA (PART 2): ANALYTICS

                    KEY POINTS
198 | Learning Engineering Toolkit

    People now do not actually look
 through telescopes. Instead, they are
“looking” through large-scale, complex
   instruments that relay data to data
 centers, and only then do they look at
  the information on their computers.
